    local code = [[
    void mainImage(out vec4 fragColor, in vec2 fragCoord)
    {
        vec2 uv = fragCoord / iResolution.xy;
        float r = 0.5 + 0.5 * sin(uv.x * 10.0 + iTime);
        float g = 0.5 + 0.5 * cos(uv.y * 10.0 + iTime);
        float b = 0.5 + 0.5 * sin((uv.x + uv.y) * 5.0 + iTime * 2.0);
        fragColor = vec4(r, g, b, 1.0);
    }
    ]]

    myShader = shadertoy.convert(code)
end

function love.update(dt)
    myShader:update(dt)
end

function love.draw()
    myShader:apply()
        love.graphics.rectangle("fill", 0, 0, 800, 600)
    myShader:clear()
end

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
1. Syntax conversion: the library automatically converts ShaderToy syntax to LÖVE-compatible format.
  2. Texture channels: iChannel0-3 correspond to LÖVE texture units.
  3. Audio processing: audio channels generate FFT and waveform textures in real time.
  4. Performance optimization: multi-pass rendering uses ping-pong buffer techniques.

This library makes using ShaderToy shaders in LÖVE simple and direct, supporting most ShaderToy features and syntax.

Reminder: this library cannot guarantee 100% success for all shaders. Some shaders are hacky, designed to exploit floating point precision to create amazing effects, and this library alone cannot perfectly reproduce them.

docs.title: 🟡 Lua Extended Library (LuaExtended)
docs.text:
Maybe you've wanted to inspect the contents of a table, but print only returns an unreadable number and still doesn't give you usable information.

This library includes many table utilities so you can inspect variables directly and efficiently.

docs.title: Remove a value from a table
docs.text:
rmVarTable(t, value, func)

  • t: target table.
  • value: value to remove.
  • func: callback invoked each time a value is removed.

Example: 

local fruits = {"apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry", "apple"}
print("Original table:", table.concat(fruits, ", "))

-- Remove all "apple" entries and call a callback for each removal
luaextended.rmVarTable(fruits, "apple", function()
    print("Removed one apple")
end)

print("After removal:", table.concat(fruits, ", "))  -- banana, cherry

docs.title: Print an entire table
docs.text:
printTable(t, indent)

  • t: target table.
  • indent: starting indentation (default is 0).

Example: 

local person = {
    name = "Alice",
    age = 30,
    address = {
        city = "Wonderland",
        zip = 12345
    },
    hobbies = {"reading", "chess"}
}
-- Create a recursive reference
person.self = person

luaextended.printTable(person)

Output: 

table (rows=4) {
    name = Alice
    age = 30
    address = 
    table (rows=2) {
        city = Wonderland
        zip = 12345
    }
    hobbies = 
    table (rows=2) {
        1 = reading
        2 = chess
    }
    self = <recursive reference>
}

docs.title: Find a value in a table
docs.text:
findTableValue(t, value)

  • t: target table.
  • value: value to search for.

Example: 

local luaextended = require("luaextended")

local config = {
    level = 5,
    settings = {
        volume = 0.8,
        difficulty = "hard"
    },
    players = {"Alice", "Bob"}
}

print(luaextended.findTableValue(config, "hard"))   --> true
print(luaextended.findTableValue(config, "easy"))   --> false
print(luaextended.findTableValue(config, 0.8))      --> true
print(luaextended.findTableValue(config, "Alice"))  --> true

docs.title: Merge two tables
docs.text:
patchTable(t, patch)

  • t: target table.
  • patch: patch table.

Example: 

local luaextended = require("luaextended")

local default = {
    name = "Default",
    version = 1.0,
    features = {
        sound = true,
        graphics = "low"
    }
}

local update = {
    version = 1.1,
    features = {
        graphics = "high"
    },
    newField = "added"
}

luaextended.patchTable(default, update)

-- Print the modified default table
luaextended.printTable(default)

Output: 

table (rows=3) {
    name = Default
    version = 1.1
    features = 
    table (rows=2) {
        sound = true
        graphics = high
    }
    newField = added
}

docs.title: JSON Library (dkjson)
docs.text: dkjson is a pure Lua implementation of JSON encoding and decoding. This library can be used together with the SafeJson library.

docs.title: Encoding (Encode)
docs.text: Simply put, encoding means structuring data and storing it in a .json file.

docs.title: Basic Encoding
docs.text:
local json = require("dkjson")

-- Encode a table
local data = {
    name = "Alice",
    age = 25,
    hobbies = {"reading", "swimming"},
    address = {
        city = "Beijing",
        zipcode = "100000"
    }
}

local json_str = json.encode(data)
-- Output: {"age":25,"hobbies":["reading","swimming"],"name":"Alice","address":{"city":"Beijing","zipcode":"100000"}}

docs.title: Advanced Encoding
docs.text:
-- Pretty-printed formatted output
local formatted = json.encode(data, {indent = true})
-- Outputs indented JSON

-- Custom key order
local ordered_data = {
    name = "Bob",
    age = 30,
    email = "bob@example.com"
}
setmetatable(ordered_data, {
    __jsonorder = {"name", "email", "age"}  -- specify output order
})

-- Custom encoding function
local custom_obj = {x = 10, y = 20}
setmetatable(custom_obj, {
    __tojson = function(self, state)
        return string.format('{"type":"point","x":%d,"y":%d}', self.x, self.y)
    end
})

docs.title: Decoding (Decode)
docs.text: Decode JSON from a .json file into usable Lua data structures.

docs.title: Basic Decoding
docs.text:
local json_str = '{"name":"Charlie","scores":[85,92,78],"active":true}'
local data, pos, err = json.decode(json_str)

if err then
    print("Decode error:", err)
else
    print(data.name)        -- Charlie
    print(data.scores[1])   -- 85
    print(data.active)      -- true
end

docs.title: Advanced Decoding
docs.text:
-- Handle null values
local json_str = '{"value":null,"items":[1,null,3]}'
local data = json.decode(json_str, nil, "NIL_VALUE")
print(data.value)       -- "NIL_VALUE" (custom null replacement)

-- Decode with metatables
local obj_meta = {__index = {type = "object"}}
local arr_meta = {__index = {type = "array"}}
local data = json.decode(json_str, nil, nil, obj_meta, arr_meta)

docs.title: String Handling
docs.text:
-- Quote a string
local str = 'Hello "World"!
New line.'
local quoted = json.quotestring(str)
-- Output: "Hello "World"!
New line."

-- Escape special characters
local special = "Line1
Line2   Tab\Backslash"
print(json.encode(special))

docs.title: Safe JSON Detection (SafeJson)
docs.text: This library is mainly used to guard against malicious code inside .json files.

docs.title: Safe JSON Parsing
docs.text:
Use safejson.parse(str) to parse a JSON string. Note: this library does not read files directly; it only parses the string content.

-- Basic parsing
local json_str = '{"name": "Alice", "age": 25, "hobbies": ["reading", "swimming"]}'
local data, err, err_type = safejson.parse(json_str)

if err then
    print("Parse failed:", err, "Error type:", err_type)
else
    print("Name:", data.name)        -- Alice
    print("Age:", data.age)         -- 25
    print("Hobby 1:", data.hobbies[1]) -- reading
end

docs.title: JSON Encoding (Same as dkjson)
docs.text:
local user_data = {
    name = "Bob",
    age = 30,
    preferences = {
        theme = "dark",
        language = "zh-CN"
    }
}

local json_output = safejson.encode(user_data)
print(json_output)
-- Output: {"name":"Bob","age":30,"preferences":{"theme":"dark","language":"zh-CN"}}

docs.title: Advanced Configuration Options
docs.text:
local options = {
    -- Dangerous keyword blacklist (extensible)
    badwords = {
        ["__index"] = true,
        ["coroutine"] = true,
        -- add custom dangerous words
        ["malicious"] = true,
    },

    -- Resource limits
    max_depth = 50,           -- maximum nesting depth
    max_items = 5000,         -- maximum number of items
    max_string_length = 50000, -- maximum string length

    -- Feature switches
    enable_precheck = true    -- enable precheck
}

local json_str = '{"data": "some content"}'
local data, err = safejson.parse(json_str, options)

docs.title: Connecting Your Game to the GameJolt Website
Create your game on the GameJolt website
docs.text:
1. First go to the GameJolt game publishing page. Choose one of these three options based on your current development stage.
    • Devlog-Only: You don't have playable content yet, but you want to share your development progress (such as images, videos, posts, or other materials).
    • Early Access: Your game has playable content but isn't complete yet; you will continue updating it.
    • Complete/Stable: Your game is finished, and you only plan to fix bugs, optimize, or make small updates.

If you just want to test, I recommend choosing the first option. It gives you a perfect personal space. 2. This shows the options after selecting Devlog-Only.

  • Title: Your game name, which appears on GameJolt and forms the first impression.
  • URL Path: Your game path. Choose an appropriate English path; combined with the random ID, this becomes your game page URL.
  • Website(optional): If your game has an official website (for example, an AU page or a dedicated game site), enter it here.
  • Engine/Language/Creation Tool: The engine/framework used to create the game. Note: SoulEngine is not listed here, so do not keep looking for it. Choose LOVE instead, because LOVE is the birthplace of SoulEngine. Of course, if you want to write SoulEngine by hand, that's fine too.
  • Add to partner system?: If enabled, you can earn revenue through GameJolt's share system. The more fans you have, the more energy orbs you charge, and the more stickers you can get. However, you must first apply to become a GameJolt creator.

Then click Save & Next to proceed to the next page. 3. Fill in the Description, Design, and Maturity fields yourself. After that, click Save Draft to reach this page: 4. Click Game API in the top menu to open this page:

This is the area that the GameJoltAPI library handles.

docs.title: Connect the game to GameJolt in code
docs.text:
Add the following code in main.lua at the start of your game to connect to GameJolt.

local gamejolt = require("GamejoltAPI")

-- Initialize the API (requires GameJolt-provided app_id and private_key)
local success = gamejolt.init(123456, "your_private_key_here")
if not success then
    print("Initialization failed: missing app_id or private_key")
    return
end

After this, if the game hiccups slightly during startup, it means the connection is being established.

docs.title: How to find your token
docs.text:
1. Click your avatar in the upper right corner (or wherever it appears).
  2. Find the Game Token option and click it to view your token.

docs.title: User login
docs.text:
After the connection is established, the game needs to link to the player's account. The player must enter their username and token to log in using gamejolt.auth_user(name, token):

Warning: do not ask for passwords inside the game. GameJolt explicitly states that requesting passwords is illegal.

local success, error = gamejolt.auth_user("myusername", "myusertoken")
if success then
    print("User authentication successful")
    -- After authentication, you can perform other operations that require login
else
    print("Authentication failed: " .. error)
end

docs.title: Fetch user information
docs.text:
If you're making an online game and want to see how many friends a player has, you can query that player's information using gamejolt.fetch_user(id, name): 

-- Player enters a friend's username to search
local friend_name = "friend_name_to_search"
local friend_data, error = gamejolt.fetch_user(nil, friend_name)
if friend_data then
    -- Show friend information and provide an option to add them
    -- This function doesn't exist by default; define a suitable one yourself
    show_friend_profile(friend_data)
else
    -- This function also doesn't exist by default; define your own
    show_message("User not found")
end

You can also fetch information after login:

-- After successful authentication, get full user information
local auth_success = gamejolt.auth_user("username", "token")
if auth_success then
    local user_info, error = gamejolt.fetch_user(nil, "username")
    if user_info then
        -- Check account status (for example, whether the email is verified)
        if user_info.status == "Active" then
            proceed_to_main_game()
        else
            show_verification_required_message()
        end
    end
end

docs.title: Add trophies to your game
docs.text:
On the Game API page, click Trophies to enter the trophy interface. There are four trophy tiers:

  • Bronze: the lowest-tier trophy, usually the easiest to obtain (for example, login or rename).
  • Silver: the third-tier trophy, usually achievable with some effort (for example, beating Sans).
  • Gold: the second-tier trophy, usually requires greater skill (for example, a no-medicine Sans fight).
  • Platinum: the highest-tier trophy, typically very difficult (for example, no-damage Sans fight or finding all secrets and completing everything).

Note: regardless of difficulty, the designer decides the trophy conditions and the code decides how they are awarded.

When adding a trophy, provide a square image, then enter a name and a short description.

docs.title: Fetch player achievements
docs.text:
When a player logs in, you can fetch all the achievements they have unlocked and display them in the game.

-- Fetch all achievements
local achievements, error = gamejolt.fetch_achievements(false)
if achievements then
    for i, trophy in ipairs(achievements.trophies) do
        print("Achievement: " .. trophy.title .. " - Status: " .. (trophy.achieved and "Unlocked" or "Locked"))
    end
end

-- Fetch only unlocked achievements
local unlocked_achievements, error = gamejolt.fetch_achievements(true)

docs.title: Unlock an achievement
docs.text:
For this, you need the trophy ID from GameJolt, which is usually six digits.

local success, error = gamejolt.unlock_achievement(123456)
if success then
    print("Achievement unlocked successfully!")
else
    print("Achievement unlock failed: " .. error)
end

docs.title: Sessions
docs.text: If a player opens the game, you can choose to view how many players are online and the total playtime.

docs.title: Example display
docs.text:
This is the session overview when a game is first created:

If Sessions are enabled and a player plays for a while and then quits, it looks like this: There are 0 active sessions (no one currently has the game open), the game has been played for 57 seconds total, the average session length is 28 seconds, and one player has played the game.

You can infer that 28 seconds means the same player played two separate sessions.

docs.title: Open a session
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.session_open() to start a session.

local result, error = gamejolt.session_open()
if result then
    print("Session opened successfully")
else
    print("Session open failed: " .. error)
end

docs.title: Keep the session alive with heartbeats
docs.text:
After a session opens, GameJolt may consider it a timed-out session unless you periodically send an activity signal to let GameJolt know the session is still active. This resets the timeout.

Because each session consumes GameJolt server resources, you must keep sending updates to keep the session valid.

Use gamejolt.session_ping(status) to maintain session activity.

-- Default state is active
-- This heartbeat can be sent every two minutes
local result, error = gamejolt.session_ping("active")
if result then
    print("Session heartbeat sent successfully")
end

-- If the player briefly leaves the game (for example by pausing), you can send idle instead without affecting the session
gamejolt.session_ping("idle")

docs.title: Close a session
docs.text:
When closing the game, add gamejolt.session_close() in love.quit to tell GameJolt the game has ended and the session no longer needs updates.

local result, error = gamejolt.session_close()
if result then
    print("Session closed successfully")
else
    print("Session close failed: " .. error)
end

docs.title: Data Storage System (DataStore)
docs.text: If a player switches to another device (for example a different computer or phone), you can upload their local storage to the GameJolt cloud so they can continue using their previous saved data.

docs.title: Store data
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.data_store(key, data, user_data) to save data.

-- Save global game data
local success, error = gamejolt.data_store("level_progress", "level_5_completed", false)
if success then
    print("Data stored successfully")
end

-- Save user-specific data
local success, error = gamejolt.data_store("player_settings", "{"sound":true,"music":false}", true)

Of course, SoulEngine does not need to manually build JSON strings like this; use the dkjson library instead. Jump to this page to see how to use the dkjson library

docs.title: Fetch data
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.data_fetch(key, user_data) to retrieve data.

-- Fetch global data
local data, error = gamejolt.data_fetch("level_progress", false)
if data then
    print("Fetched data: " .. data)
end

-- Fetch user-specific data
local user_data, error = gamejolt.data_fetch("player_settings", true)

Or use gamejolt.data_fetch_all(user_data) to retrieve all data.

-- Fetch all global data keys
local all_data, error = gamejolt.data_fetch_all(false)
if all_data then
    for i, key_info in ipairs(all_data.keys) do
        print("Data key: " .. key_info.key)
    end
end

Use gamejolt.data_get_keys(user_data) to get all keys stored in the data.

local keys, error = gamejolt.data_get_keys(true)
if keys then
    for i, key in ipairs(keys) do
        print("Key: " .. key)
    end
end

docs.title: Delete data
docs.text:
When a player chooses to RESET or otherwise clear data, use gamejolt.data_delete(key, user_data) to delete an entry.

local success, error = gamejolt.data_delete("temp_data", true)
if success then
    print("Data deleted successfully")
end

docs.title: Scores / Leaderboard System (Scores)
docs.text: If you're developing a rhythm game or an UNDERTALE-style game and want a leaderboard, use the score system to record and upload player scores to GameJolt.

docs.title: Fetch local scores (no user login required)
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.fetch_scores_local(table_id) to get local scores.

local scores, error = gamejolt.fetch_scores_local(123)  -- 123 is the score table ID
if scores then
    for i, score in ipairs(scores.scores) do
        print("Score: " .. score.score .. " - User: " .. score.user)
    end
end

docs.title: Fetch global leaderboard scores (user login required)
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.fetch_scores_global(table_id) to get the global leaderboard.

local scores, error = gamejolt.fetch_scores_global(123)
if scores then
    for i, score in ipairs(scores.scores) do
        print("Score: " .. score.score .. " - Sort value: " .. score.sort)
    end
end

docs.title: Submit a score to the leaderboard
docs.text:
Use gamejolt.submit_score(score, table_id, sort, extra_data) to submit a score.

local success, error = gamejolt.submit_score(1500, 123, 1500, "Clear time: 2:30")
if success then
    print("Score submitted successfully")
else
    print("Score submission failed: " .. error)
end

extra_data refers to additional submission details, as shown above with Clear time: 2:30. It can also be something like: "Time:2:30|Difficulty:Hard|Stage:5".

docs.title: TimeManager
docs.text: Implements a coroutine-based timer management system, primarily used for creating and managing awaitable code blocks.

docs.title: Create a code block
docs.text:
Use timer.CreateBlock(func, name, group) to create and start an awaitable code block.

-- Create an awaitable code block
local myTimer = timer.CreateBlock(function()
    print("Starting execution")

    wait(2)  -- wait 2 seconds
    print("Executed after 2 seconds")

    wait.frame(60)  -- wait 1 second (60 frames)
    print("Executed after 1 second")
end, "Example Timer", "UI Group")

docs.title: Wait by seconds
docs.text:
timer.CreateBlock(function()
    wait(3.5)  -- wait 3.5 seconds
    print("Executed after 3.5 seconds")
end)

docs.title: Wait by frames
docs.text:
timer.CreateBlock(function()
    wait.frame(30)  -- wait 30 frames (assuming 60FPS, i.e. 0.5 seconds)
    print("Executed after half a second")
end)

docs.title: Wait by condition
docs.text:
local canProceed = false
timer.CreateBlock(function()
    wait.ntl(function() return canProceed end)
    print("Condition met, continuing execution")
end)

docs.title: Group management
docs.text:
-- Create multiple timers in the same group
timer.CreateBlock(function() wait(1) print("Timer 1") end, "timer1", "battleGroup")
timer.CreateBlock(function() wait(2) print("Timer 2") end, "timer2", "battleGroup")

-- Batch operations
timer.StopGroup("battleGroup")  -- pause all timers in the battle group
timer.ResumeGroup("battleGroup") -- resume all timers in the battle group

docs.title: Timer control
docs.text:
local timerObj = timer.CreateBlock(function()
    while true do
        print("Executed each frame")
        wait.frame(1)
    end
end)

-- Control methods
timerObj:Stop()     -- pause the timer
timerObj:Resume()   -- resume the timer
timerObj:Restart()  -- restart the timer
print("Is active:", timerObj:IsActive())
print("Progress:", timerObj:GetProgress())

docs.title: Complete usage example
docs.text:
local skillTimers = {}

function castSpell(spellName, cooldown)
    if skillTimers[spellName] and skillTimers[spellName]:IsActive() then
        print(spellName .. " is still on cooldown")
        return false
    end

    skillTimers[spellName] = timer.CreateBlock(function()
        print("Casting " .. spellName)
        wait(cooldown)
        print(spellName .. " cooldown finished")
    end, spellName .. " Cooldown", "skillGroup")

    return true
end

-- Usage example
castSpell("BigPotion", 5)  -- 5-second cooldown
castSpell("SmallPotion", 3)  -- 3-second cooldown

docs.title: Debugging and monitoring
docs.text:
-- Get system status
local debugInfo = timer.GetDebugInfo()
print(string.format("Active timers: %d/%d, group count: %d", 
    debugInfo.activeTimers, 
    debugInfo.totalTimers, 
    debugInfo.groupCount))

docs.title: GUI Library (GUIManager)
docs.text:
Maybe your player needs to click a button in the game, or click an image to open a link? Or maybe you just want to display something without caring what the player clicks?

You can do all of that with the gui library.

docs.title: Create buttons
docs.text:
This library provides four types of buttons:

  • Rectangle button: gui.add_button_rect(x, y, width, height, text, callback, opts) 

    local myButton = gui.add_button_rect(100, 100, 120, 40, "Click Me",
    function(btn)
        print("Button clicked!")
    end,
    {font = love.graphics.newFont(14)} -- optional parameters
)

  • Rounded rectangle button: gui.add_button_roundrect(x, y, width, height, radius, text, callback, opts) 

    local roundButton = gui.add_button_roundrect(250, 100, 120, 40, 10, "Rounded",
    function() print("Rounded button triggered") end
)

  • Ellipse button: gui.add_button_ellipse(x, y, width, height, text, callback, opts) 

    local ellipseButton = gui.add_button_ellipse(400, 100, 100, 50, "Ellipse",
    function() print("Ellipse button triggered") end
)

  • Image button: gui.add_button_image(x, y, image, callback, opts) 

    local imgButton = gui.add_button_image(550, 100, "icon.png",
    function() print("Image button triggered") end
)

docs.title: Modify creation parameters
docs.text:
You may not like the default button font, or the font might be fuzzy or too small, or the colors may not be what you want. You may want a gradient background. That's all possible through the final opts parameter.

Here are some of the things opts can do:

  • font: font, size, or which font to use. 

    local myFont = love.graphics.newFont(18) -- create an 18-point font
local button = gui.add_button_rect(100, 100, 150, 40, "Large Button",
    function() print("Clicked") end,
    {
        font = myFont -- use a custom font
    }
)

  • draw: custom button drawing 

    local button = gui.add_button_rect(100, 200, 150, 40, "Custom Gradient",
    function() print("Clicked") end,
    {
        font = love.graphics.newFont(14),
        draw = function(b)
            -- Save current color
            local r, g, b, a = love.graphics.getColor()
            -- Draw a horizontal gradient background
            for i = 0, b.width do
                local ratio = i / b.width
                love.graphics.setColor(0.2, 0.2, 0.8, 1 * ratio) -- blue gradient
                love.graphics.line(b.x + i, b.y, b.x + i, b.y + b.height)
            end
            -- Restore color and draw text
            love.graphics.setColor(1, 1, 1, 1) -- white text
            love.graphics.setFont(b.font)
            love.graphics.printf(b.text, b.x, b.y + 10, b.width, "center")
            -- Restore original color
            love.graphics.setColor(r, g, b, a)
        end
    }
)

  • colors: color set; can change normal, hover, pressed, disabled, and text colors. 

    local button = gui.add_button_rect(100, 250, 150, 40, "Color Button",
    function() print("Clicked") end,
    {
        font = love.graphics.newFont(14),
        colors = {
            normal = {0.1, 0.7, 0.3},   -- green
            hover = {0.2, 0.8, 0.4},    -- bright green
            pressed = {0.0, 0.6, 0.2},  -- dark green
            disabled = {0.5, 0.5, 0.5}, -- gray
            font = {1, 1, 1}            -- white text
        }
    }
)

  • enabled: set the button's initial enabled/disabled state. 

    local button = gui.add_button_rect(100, 300, 150, 40, "Disabled Button",
    function() print("This callback will not trigger") end,
    {
        enabled = false -- start disabled
    }
)

docs.title: Create a slider
docs.text:
Use gui.add_slider(x, y, width, height, min, max, value, callback, step, orientation, opts). 

local volumeSlider = gui.add_slider(100, 180, 200, 20, 0, 100, 50,
    function(value)
        setVolume(value / 100) -- assume this is your volume setter
    end,
    1, -- step size, adjust by 1 unit each movement
    "horizontal",
    {show_value = true} -- optional: display current value next to the slider
)

docs.title: Create a text input box
docs.text:
Use gui.add_text_input(x, y, width, height, placeholder, callback, opts) to create a text input field. 

local nameInput = gui.add_text_input(100, 230, 200, 30, "Enter player name",
    function(text, isReturnPressed)
        if isReturnPressed then
            print("Player confirmed name: " .. text)
        else
            -- handle input live, for example validation
        end
    end,
    {font = love.graphics.newFont(12)}
)

docs.title: Create a table panel
docs.text:
Use gui.add_table(x, y, w, h, headers, config) to create a visual table. 

local scoreTable = gui.add_table(400, 180, 300, 200,
    {"Rank", "Player", "Score"}, -- table headers
    {colors = {header_bg = {0.2, 0.4, 0.7}}} -- optional config
)
-- Add rows
scoreTable:add_row({1, "PlayerOne", 1500})
scoreTable:add_row({2, "PlayerTwo", 1200})

docs.title: Create windows
docs.text:
Use gui.add_window(x, y, w, h, title, opts) to create a window. 

local settingsWindow = gui.add_window(200, 150, 350, 250, "Settings Panel", {
    closable = true,
    minimizable = true,
    maximizable = true,
    onclose = function(win) print("Window closed") end
})
-- Add child controls inside the window (relative to the window client area)
local innerButton = settingsWindow:add_child(
    gui._create_button{x=20, y=30, width=100, height=30, text="Inner Button"}
)

docs.title: Create a dropdown menu
docs.text:
Use gui.add_menu(x, y, w, h, items, functions) to create a dropdown menu. 

local fileMenu = gui.add_menu(50, 50, 80, 25,
    {"New", "Open", "Save", "Exit"}, -- menu items
    { -- corresponding callbacks
        function() print("New file") end,
        function() print("Open file") end,
        function() print("Save file") end,
        function() love.event.quit() end
    }
)

docs.title: Window Management (Windows)
docs.text: Note: this page is not supported on Linux or macOS, and it may never be supported.

docs.title: Get the window handle
docs.text:
Use window.getHandle() to get the window handle.

local hwnd = window.getHandle()

docs.title: Process window messages
docs.text:
Use window.processMessages() to process the system window message queue. It should be called regularly in the main loop.

function love.update(dt)
    -- Process Windows messages to prevent the window from becoming unresponsive
    if window.isWindows() then
        window.processMessages()
    end
end

docs.title: Window transparency
docs.text:
Use window.setTransparency(hwnd, alpha) to set the transparency of a window.

function love.keypressed(key)
    if key == "t" then
        local hwnd = window.getHandle()
        if hwnd then
            -- Set the window to semi-transparent (128/255)
            window.setTransparency(hwnd, 128)
        end
    elseif key == "o" then
        local hwnd = window.getHandle()
        if hwnd then
            -- Restore opacity
            window.setTransparency(hwnd, 255)
        end
    end
end

docs.title: Background transparency
docs.text:
Use window.setBackgroundTransparent(hwnd, colorKey, alpha) to make the background transparent.

window.setBackgroundTransparent(hwnd, 0x000000, 0) -- make all black transparent

Note: this function can be used to create the effect of characters running out of the window.

docs.title: Screenshot
docs.text:
Use window.saveScreenshot(x, y, w, h, path, throughWindow) to capture a specific area.

function love.keypressed(key)
    if key == "p" then
        -- Capture the entire window
        local success, message = window.saveScreenshot(0, 0, 800, 600, "screenshot.bmp", true)

        if success then
            print("Screenshot saved successfully")
        else
            print("Screenshot failed:", message)
        end

        -- Or capture a specific screen region (not through the window)
        window.saveScreenshot(100, 100, 400, 300, "area.bmp", false)
    end
end

docs.title: Show system dialog
docs.text:
Use window.showDialog(message, buttons, title) to create a message dialog.

function love.mousepressed(x, y, button)
    if button == 1 then
        local result = window.showDialog(
            "Are you sure you want to quit the game?",
            {"Yes", "No", "Cancel"},
            "Quit Confirmation"
        )

        if result == 1 then -- clicked "Yes"
            love.event.quit()
        elseif result == 2 then -- clicked "No"
            print("Continue playing")
        else -- clicked "Cancel" or other
            print("Action canceled")
        end
    end
end

docs.title: Online Development (sock)
docs.text: Maybe you've thought about... playing a game with friends? The networking library can make that dream come true.

docs.title: How networking works
docs.text:
Before you start development, let’s look at how two computers connect. Think about the game MINECRAFT and how it does networking.

Clearly, when entering a server address, most of the time we see it represented like this:

123.456.789.012:25565
mc.hypixel.net

The common point between these addresses is that they use the format [IP address]:[port].

You may have noticed that mc.hypixel.net appears to have no port, but in fact its port defaults to 25565, so it is equivalent to mc.hypixel.net:25565.

Putting Minecraft aside, the things needed for networking are:

  • IP address: like the location of a building; it locates the computer running the server.
  • Port number: like the room number inside that building; it tells the computer which program should receive the data.

So our network address still follows this format. When you want to join someone else’s room later, ask for both the IP and the port number, then add them to join the game.

docs.title: Networking challenges
docs.text:
If you really want to create a multiplayer UT-style game (for example, utmv, DFC, etc.), the first challenge you will encounter is latency.

Because latency exists, the feel of games that require precise positional control (such as FPS) can degrade sharply. There are many remedies for this:

In public-network games, code-level approaches to fight latency mainly include prediction, interpolation, lag compensation, protocol optimization, and state synchronization. Developers have invented many advanced techniques, such as Quake’s client-side prediction and Valve’s lag compensation, to improve game experience.

Method Category Specific Technique Description Client prediction Input prediction The client responds immediately to local player input while sending the input to the server for validation and synchronization, reducing perceived delay. Interpolation Entity interpolation The client smooths the positions of other players or objects based on server snapshots to eliminate stuttering. Lag compensation Backward reconciliation The server processes events by rewinding time to match the game state when the client sent input, ensuring fairness. Protocol optimization Use UDP instead of TCP Custom UDP protocols (such as reliable UDP) reduce connection overhead and retransmission latency, suitable for real-time games. State synchronization Deterministic lockstep All clients run identical logic and only sync inputs, reducing network traffic but requiring high consistency. Server architecture Authoritative server The server is the single authority for game state; clients only display approximate results to prevent cheating and synchronization issues. Bandwidth optimization Compression and priority Compress network packets and prioritize important updates (such as position updates) to reduce latency impact.

docs.title: In detail
docs.text:
• Client prediction: allows the client to predict the result of player actions, such as movement or shooting, then reconcile with the server state. If the server corrects the state, the client smooths the adjustment, which reduces perceived input delay.

  • Interpolation: the client stores entity state snapshots received from the server and interpolates positions during rendering so other players’ movement appears smooth. For example, Source engine interpolation computes intermediate states based on timestamps to prevent teleporting.

  • Lag compensation: when the server receives a client event, it considers network delay and rewinds the game world state for processing. For example, in shooting games, the server calculates the historical position of a player at the moment a shot was fired, ensuring that high-latency players can still hit their targets. Valve’s algorithm is a classic example.

  • Protocol optimization: games often use custom UDP protocols (such as ENet or custom implementations) with reliability mechanisms (like sequence numbers and acknowledgements) to avoid TCP head-of-line blocking. Developers have created snapshot protocols to synchronize state efficiently.

  • State synchronization: approaches include deterministic lockstep (suitable for turn-based or RTS games) and snapshot interpolation (suitable for FPS). Lockstep requires all clients to stay in sync, while snapshot interpolation lets clients interpolate between updates after receiving them.

  • Server architecture: use an authoritative server model where the server validates all critical actions and prevents client cheating. Combining regional servers or edge computing (such as AWS GameLift) can reduce physical latency.

  • Bandwidth optimization: reduce latency by compressing data (such as delta compression that only sends changed parts), using priority queues (send important updates first), and adaptive update rates (adjust send frequency based on network conditions).

docs.title: What to say
docs.text: Of course, I am glad someone would use SoulEngine to develop an FPS-style game, but I feel it is a bit unfair to the developers. This engine is best suited for lightweight RPGs.

docs.title: Example demonstration
docs.text:
You may know this library exists, but still not be sure how to use it to make an online game.

No problem. I will give an example here, but note that it does not include any data encryption. To prevent cheating, the developer must implement encryption themselves.

if (love.system.getOS() == "Windows") then
        local handle = io.popen("chcp 65001", "r")
        handle:close()
    end

local sock = require "sock"

function love.load()
    local PORT = 51234
    local isServer = false
    local client = nil
    local server = nil

    -- 1. First try to create a server
    local serverSuccess, serverObj = pcall(sock.newServer, "localhost", PORT)
    if serverSuccess then
        isServer = true
        server = serverObj
        print("[Startup Mode] Server mode (port: " .. PORT .. ")")

        -- Server stores all client states in a table
        server.allClientsState = {}

        server:on("connect", function(data, clientPeer)
            local clientId = clientPeer:getConnectId()
            print("[Server] Client #" .. clientId .. " connected.")

            -- Generate a random color and assign an ID for the new client
            local newClientInfo = {
                id = clientId,
                color = { love.math.random(), love.math.random(), love.math.random(), 1 },
                x = 400,
                y = 300
            }
            -- Store it in the server state table
            server.allClientsState[clientId] = newClientInfo

            -- Send a welcome message with assigned info
            clientPeer:send("welcome", {
                yourId = clientId,
                yourColor = newClientInfo.color
            })

            -- Immediately broadcast the current global state to everyone (including the new client)
            broadcastStateToAll()
        end)

        server:on("disconnect", function(data, clientPeer)
            local clientId = clientPeer:getConnectId()
            print("[Server] Client #" .. clientId .. " disconnected.")
            -- Remove from the state table
            server.allClientsState[clientId] = nil
            -- Broadcast the updated state
            broadcastStateToAll()
        end)

        -- Server listens for client state updates
        server:on("clientUpdate", function(data, clientPeer)
            local clientId = clientPeer:getConnectId()
            if server.allClientsState[clientId] then
                -- Update the client's position
                server.allClientsState[clientId].x = data.x
                server.allClientsState[clientId].y = data.y
                -- Immediately broadcast the global state to everyone
                broadcastStateToAll()
            end
        end)

        -- Helper function to broadcast global state
        function broadcastStateToAll()
            -- Send a copy of the state to avoid reference issues
            local stateCopy = {}
            for id, info in pairs(server.allClientsState) do
                stateCopy[id] = { x = info.x, y = info.y, color = info.color }
            end
            server:sendToAll("worldState", stateCopy)
        end

    else
        -- Server creation failed, switch to client mode
        isServer = false
        print("[Startup Mode] Client mode (connecting to server...)")
        client = sock.newClient("localhost", PORT)

        -- Client local state
        client.myId = nil
        client.myColor = nil
        client.myImage = nil
        client.mouseX, client.mouseY = 400, 300

        -- Store the global state received from the server
        client.worldState = {}
        -- Timer to control state update frequency
        client.stateUpdateTimer = 0
        client.stateUpdateInterval = 0 -- send each frame by default, but this can be adjusted to 0.05 seconds if needed

        client:on("connect", function(data)
            print("[Client] Connected to server.")
            -- Load the image after connection
            client.myImage = love.graphics.newImage("image.png")
        end)

        client:on("welcome", function(data)
            print("[Client] Received assigned ID: " .. data.yourId)
            client.myId = data.yourId
            client.myColor = data.yourColor
        end)

        client:on("worldState", function(data)
            -- Update the local copy of the global state received from the server
            client.worldState = data
        end)

        client:on("disconnect", function(data)
            print("[Client] Disconnected from server.")
        end)

        client:connect()
    end

    G_IS_SERVER = isServer
    G_CLIENT = client
    G_SERVER = server
end

function love.update(dt)
    if G_IS_SERVER and G_SERVER then
        G_SERVER:update()
    elseif G_CLIENT then
        G_CLIENT:update()
        -- Update local mouse position
        G_CLIENT.mouseX, G_CLIENT.mouseY = love.mouse.getPosition()

        -- Periodically send local position updates to the server
        if G_CLIENT.myId then -- ensure we have an ID
            G_CLIENT.stateUpdateTimer = G_CLIENT.stateUpdateTimer + dt
            if G_CLIENT.stateUpdateTimer >= G_CLIENT.stateUpdateInterval then
                G_CLIENT.stateUpdateTimer = 0
                G_CLIENT:send("clientUpdate", { x = G_CLIENT.mouseX, y = G_CLIENT.mouseY })
            end
        end
    end
end

function love.draw()
    if G_IS_SERVER then
        love.graphics.setColor(1, 1, 1)
        love.graphics.print("Server running (port: 51234)", 10, 10)
        love.graphics.print("Client count: " .. (G_SERVER and tostring(G_SERVER:getClientCount()) or "0"), 10, 30)
        love.graphics.print("Syncing all client positions in real time...", 10, 50)

        -- Optional server visualization of all client positions
        for id, info in pairs(G_SERVER.allClientsState or {}) do
            love.graphics.setColor(info.color[1], info.color[2], info.color[3])
            love.graphics.circle("fill", info.x, info.y, 20)
            love.graphics.setColor(1,1,1)
            love.graphics.print("Client#"..id, info.x - 20, info.y - 40)
        end
    elseif G_CLIENT then
        -- Client view: draw all client images based on the global state
        if G_CLIENT.myImage and G_CLIENT.worldState then
            local img = G_CLIENT.myImage
            local w, h = img:getDimensions()

            -- Draw all clients received from the server
            for clientId, clientInfo in pairs(G_CLIENT.worldState) do
                love.graphics.setColor(clientInfo.color[1], clientInfo.color[2], clientInfo.color[3], 1)
                love.graphics.draw(img, clientInfo.x, clientInfo.y, 0, 1, 1, w/2, h/2)

                -- Optionally display the ID next to the image
                love.graphics.setColor(1, 1, 1)
                love.graphics.print("#" .. clientId, clientInfo.x - 10, clientInfo.y + h/2 + 5)
            end
        end

        -- Display local client info
        love.graphics.setColor(1, 1, 1)
        love.graphics.print("Client (connected)", 10, 10)
        if G_CLIENT.myId then
            love.graphics.print("My ID: " .. G_CLIENT.myId, 10, 30)
        end
    end
end

function love.quit()
    if G_CLIENT and G_CLIENT:isConnected() then
        G_CLIENT:disconnect()
    end
end

docs.title: Architecture and design analysis
docs.text:
This example implements a simple P2P position synchronization demo. Its core design is:

  1. Dynamic role determination: At startup, the program first tries to create a server on the specified port. If successful (port is free), it becomes the server. If it fails (usually because a server is already running on that port), it automatically switches to client mode and connects to localhost.
  2. Authoritative server: The server is the only authority for all game state. It is responsible for:
    • Assigning a unique ID and color to each connected client.
    • Maintaining a global table of all client states (allClientsState).
    • Receiving client position updates and immediately broadcasting the consolidated global state to all clients.
  3. Client simulation and synchronization: The client is responsible for:
    • Simulating its own character based on local input (here, mouse position).
    • Periodically sending its simulated state (position) to the server for validation and synchronization.
    • Receiving and applying the authoritative global state broadcast by the server, then rendering all characters.

docs.title: Core mechanisms explained
docs.text:
1. Event-driven connection handling

    • server:on(eventName, callback) / client:on(eventName, callback) is the core API of sock.lua. It registers listeners for network events like "connect", "disconnect", or custom events like "welcome" and "clientUpdate". The callback runs when the event triggers.
    • Key point: Event listeners must be set before calling client:connect() or before the connection begins, otherwise you may miss events.

  2. State synchronization flow

    • Client -> Server: The client sends a custom event with client:send("clientUpdate", {x, y}).
    • Server processing and broadcast: The server updates its state in the "clientUpdate" listener and calls broadcastStateToAll to send the new global state to everyone.
    • Server -> All clients: All clients receive the update in the "worldState" listener and update their local worldState table for rendering.
    • Why send a state copy? The broadcastStateToAll function creates stateCopy to avoid sending a direct reference to allClientsState. This prevents unpredictable bugs if clients accidentally modify received data. It is a defensive programming practice.

  3. Resource allocation timing

    • Note that the client does not load image.png immediately in love.load. The image is loaded in the "connect" callback after the network connection is established (client.myImage = love.graphics.newImage("image.png")). This is generally better logic.

docs.title: Practical advice
docs.text:
• Debugging: Use print to log connection, disconnect, send, and receive events. It's the most basic and effective method for debugging network code.
  • Security: This example has no security measures. Any client can send arbitrary data. In a real game, the server must validate all received client data to prevent cheating.
  • Extension: You can easily extend the data sent in clientUpdate and worldState to include more state, such as velocity, animation frame, health, etc.

By understanding these key points, developers can clearly grasp this example's workflow and build more stable and complex networked game features on top of it.

docs.title: Encrypting data
docs.text:
As mentioned above, you may worry about cheating. The library includes binser.lua to help with serialization operations.

But note that binser.lua is only a serialization/deserialization library. It does not provide any encryption by itself. You must add your own encryption layer.

-- The library includes sock and binser; make sure to require the correct path. By default they are in the Libraries/Network folder.
local binser = require 'binser'
local sock   = require 'sock'

-- Server side
function serverFunc()
    local server = sock.newServer('*', 22122)
    server:on('connect', function(data, client)
        print('Client connected:', client)
    end)
    server:on('receive', function(data, client)
        -- 4. Deserialize the received data
        local receivedData = binser.deserialize(data)
        print('Received data:', receivedData[1], receivedData[2])

        -- Prepare a response
        local response = {"Server response", 2026}
        -- 1. Serialize the data to send
        local serializedResponse = binser.serialize(response)
        -- 2. Send the serialized string
        client:send(serializedResponse)
    end)
    server:listen()
end

-- Client side
function clientFunc()
    local client = sock.newClient('localhost', 22122)
    client:on('connect', function(data)
        print('Connected to server')
        -- Prepare data to send
        local playerData = { name = "Player1", score = 100, inventory = {"Sword", "Shield"} }
        -- 1. Serialize the data
        local serializedData = binser.serialize(playerData)
        -- 2. Send the serialized string
        client:send(serializedData)
    end)
    client:on('receive', function(data)
        -- 4. Deserialize the received data
        local response = binser.deserialize(data)
        print('Server response:', response[1], response[2])
    end)
    client:connect()
end

docs.title: Cheat Engine scanning
docs.text:
We must call in our old friend, Cheat Engine.

If you don't know what Cheat Engine is, let's briefly understand its numerical strength and concept attack.

docs.title: Cheat Engine's numerical strength and conceptual properties
docs.text:
1. Powerful memory modification: CE can directly scan and modify game process memory, enabling cheats such as infinite health, infinite ammo, and one-shot kills. It can even be packaged as a standalone trainer program for distribution.

  2. Low cheating barrier and community support: CE provides a graphical interface and Lua scripting support, allowing even non-programmers to use it quickly. Its cheat table (.CT) sharing mechanism forms a large cheating community that accelerates the spread of tools.

  3. Forces developers into costly offense and defense: To counter CE, developers must deploy complex anti-cheat systems (such as Easy Anti-Cheat or BattlEye). These systems need to monitor abnormal memory access, provide driver-level protection, and enforce server-side validation, creating an ongoing and expensive arms race.

  4. Triggers multi-layer anti-cheat detection: Typical CE operations (such as frequent ReadProcessMemory/WriteProcessMemory calls and debugger attachment) are easy for anti-cheat systems to detect through behavior analysis and signature matching, which can lead to bans and user complaints.

  5. Creates high-dimensional cheat threats: CE is not only used for local single-player games; it can also attack mobile games running in PC emulators. This “dimensional reduction strike” increases the difficulty of detection and defense.

  6. Adds extra technical burden: Developers need to invest extra time in data encryption, dynamic memory addresses, multi-level pointers, and authoritative server validation to make CE modification harder, which increases development cost and maintenance complexity.

According to game security data, modifier attacks (represented by CE) once accounted for about 11% of game security risk, making it a threat developers must address.

docs.title: Can CE be prevented?
docs.text:
For the prevention we mentioned earlier — using binser.lua plus some encryption — it can stop some basic CE exploration.

For example, if you're a CE beginner, you may give up after facing it.

Why does this help? The reason is:

Raw Lua tables, numbers, and other data are stored in memory in relatively regular structures, which are easy for CE to locate through scans like "unknown initial value" -> "value changed". After serialization with binser, those values become a compact binary string before sending. In memory, the original data structure disappears and is replaced by a sequence of bytes that is not easy to interpret directly. This effectively interferes with searches based on value or structural patterns.

But unfortunately, you cannot prevent all cheating. CE is strong enough that 99.9% of developers cannot completely stop it.

But if you think about it, developing an online game to play with friends should be about having fun.

docs.title: Understanding Battle Scenes
docs.text: In SoulEngine, when the player encounters a battle, they enter a dedicated scene. All battle logic is written inside that scene.

docs.title: Battle Scene Location
docs.text:
All battle scenes are placed in the Scenes/Battle/ folder and use the scene_battle scene to build battles.

You may also notice another scene named scene_battle_origin. This is the old version of the battle scene. Its internal logic is extremely large and can easily trigger an upvalues threshold error. For this reason, scene_battle is a simplified version of scene_battle_origin. It only provides a few interfaces, but it is enough to create a full battle.

That does not mean scene_battle_origin is useless. Because it is much lower level, it is still the best place to modify the entire battle flow when you want to change the system from the ground up.

Now let's see what is inside scene_battle.

docs.title: Battle Scene Contents
docs.text:
The default code from scene_battle is shown below: 

-- This is a template for creating a new scene in the game.
-- You can use this as a starting point for your own scenes.
local SCENE = {}
local b = require("Scripts.Libraries.Battle.BattleInit")
battle = b.Init("Scripts.Libraries.Game.Encounter")
atkp = require("Scripts.Libraries.Battle.Patterns.template")
b.SetAtkPattern(atkp)

local function HandleActions(enemy_name, action)
    local battle = b.battle
    local temp_tab = {}

    -- Deep copy
    for k, v in pairs(battle.Enemies) do
        temp_tab[k] = v
    end

    if not battle or not battle.Enemies then return end
    if (enemy_name == temp_tab[1].name) then
        local enemy = battle.Enemies[1]
        if (action == enemy.actions[1]) then
            b.BattleDialogue(localize.Act11)
        elseif (action == enemy.actions[2]) then
            b.BattleDialogue(localize.Act12)
        end
    elseif (enemy_name == temp_tab[2].name) then
        local enemy = battle.Enemies[2]
        if (action == enemy.actions[1]) then
            b.BattleDialogue(localize.Act21)
        elseif (action == enemy.actions[2]) then
            b.BattleDialogue(localize.Act22)
        end
    end
end

local function HandleItems(itemID)
    local battle = b.battle
    if not battle then return end
    local inventory = battle.Inventory
    local randomText = {
        "* No."
    }

    b.BattleDialogue({
        "* You found an item...[wait:30]
  [colorRGB:255, 255, 0]" .. itemID .. "!",
        randomText[love.math.random(1, #randomText)]
    })
end

local function HandleSpare()
    b.battle.STATE = "ACTIONSELECT"
end

local nextwaves = {"wave_test1", "wave_test2", "wave_test3", "wave_test4"}
b.battle.nextwave = "wave_test1"
local waveProgress = 1
local function DefenseEnding()
    waveProgress = waveProgress + 1
    if (waveProgress > #nextwaves) then waveProgress = 1 end
    b.battle.nextwave = nextwaves[waveProgress]
end

local function OnHit(Bullet)
    local battle = b.battle
    if not battle then return end
    local mode = Bullet['HurtMode']
    if (mode == "normal" or type(mode) == "nil") then
        battle.Player.Hurt(1, 60, true)
        b.AddKR(5)
    elseif (mode == "cyan" or mode == "blue") then
        if (keyboard.GetState("arrows") > 0) then
            battle.Player.Hurt(1, 0, true)
            b.AddKR(1)
        end
    elseif (mode == "orange") then
        if (keyboard.GetState("arrows") <= 0) then
            battle.Player.Hurt(1, 0, true)
            b.AddKR(1)
        end
    elseif (mode == "green") then
        battle.Player.Heal(1)
        Bullet:Destroy()
    end
end

b.HandleActions = HandleActions
b.HandleItems   = HandleItems
b.HandleSpare   = HandleSpare
b.DefenseEnding = DefenseEnding
b.OnHit         = OnHit




-- This is a fake scene for testing purposes.
function SCENE.load()
    -- Load any resources needed for this scene here.
    -- For example, you might load images, sounds, etc.
end

-- This function is called to update the scene.
function SCENE.update(dt)
    -- Update any game logic for this scene here.
    -- For example, you might update animations, handle input, etc.
    b.Update(dt)
    if (b.GetSelectedEnemy() == 1 and b.GetState() == "ATTACKING") then
        -- print("Selected Poseur")
    end
end

-- This function is called to draw the scene.
-- It is called after the main game loop has finished updating.
function SCENE.draw()
    -- Draw the scene here.
    -- For example, you might draw images, text, etc.
    b.Draw()
end

-- This function is called when the scene is switched away from.
function SCENE.clear()
    -- Clear any resources used by this scene here.
    -- For example, you might unload images, sounds, etc.
    b.Clear()
    package.loaded["Scripts.Libraries.Battle.BattleInit"] = nil
end

-- Don't touch this(just one line).
return SCENE

docs.title: Battle System Initialization
docs.text:
local b = require("Scripts.Libraries.Battle.BattleInit")
battle = b.Init("Scripts.Libraries.Game.Encounter")
atkp = require("Scripts.Libraries.Battle.Patterns.template")
b.SetAtkPattern(atkp)
Line 1: call the battle scene framework. Line 2: specify which enemy file this battle should face. This file also includes the player's initial HP, name, and other values. (Change this when you build the encounter.) Line 3: define the player's attack type. If you do not change this manually, the default is the wooden stick / knife attack animation. Line 4: tell the battle system to use the attack pattern declared on line 3.

docs.title: Callback Functions
docs.text:
These callback functions are triggered when the player performs specific actions. Pay attention to the trigger timing below to create the effect you want:

  • HandleActions(enemy_name, action): triggered when the enemy with name enemy_name is selected and the selected action is action.
  • HandleItems(itemID): triggered when the item with ID itemID is selected.
  • HandleSpare(): triggered when the player chooses the Spare button.
  • DefenseEnding(): triggered when the game returns from the enemy turn to the player's turn.
  • OnHit(Bullet): triggered when the player collides with a projectile-style sprite where isBullet = true.

The following callbacks do not appear directly inside scene_battle, but they can still be modified:

  • HandleFlee(): triggered the instant the player successfully flees.
  • FleeUpdate(): continues to run after the player has fled successfully.

docs.title: Built-in Functions
docs.text:
Some of these functions can detect which enemy the player is attacking; some can print text directly in the battle box. Here is the full list:

  • b.BattleDialogue(texts, targetState): makes the narration type out texts, then automatically switches to targetState after the text finishes. For example, if you want to return to the four-button menu after the text, set targetState to ACTIONSELECT; if you want to go straight into battle, set it to DEFENDING.
  • b.AddKR(kramount): add KR to the player.
  • b.SetAtkPattern(pattern): set the battle attack pattern.
  • b.SetWinText(func): manually set the victory text after winning.
  • b.GetUIButtons(): retrieve the four buttons at the bottom of the screen (they are stored in a table).
  • b.GetSelectedEnemy(): get the index of the currently selected enemy.
  • b.GetSelectedEnemyName(): get the name of the currently selected enemy.
  • b.GetState(): get the current state.
  • b.WinBattleCall(): triggered at the moment of victory (this function uses the text set by SetWinText).
  • b.ToggleKR(): toggle KR on or off. If KR is turned off while the player has KR, the remaining KR is converted into HP.
  • b.EnteringStateInherited(): inherit the EnteringState function. Set this when using the scene separately.
  • b.EnemyJustDied(): This function is triggered when any enemy on the field is killed.

docs.title: Battle Scene Variables
docs.text: You may be curious how Sans's opening attack is made, how to quickly enter the first scene, or how to find enemy positions.

docs.title: Enemy Settings
docs.text: All enemy files are located in Scripts/Libraries/Game/. The player's base variables such as name and HP are also stored there.

docs.title: Battle State (STATE)
docs.text:
STATE is a relatively complex system. It has multiple values that describe the current UI state. Below are all STATE values:

A first-level state menu that can be reached with a single input:

  • ACTIONSELECT: player turn; choose one of four buttons.
  • FIGHTMENU: battle menu when the player clicks FIGHT.
  • ACTMENU: battle menu when the player clicks ACT.
  • ITEMMENU: battle menu when the player clicks ITEM.
  • MERCYMENU: battle menu when the player clicks MERCY.
  • DEFENDING: enemy turn.

A second-level state menu that is usually entered from the previous state:

  • ATTACKING: the player is aiming and timing the attack bar. This is entered from FIGHTMENU.
  • ACTSELECTING: the player has chosen an action and is selecting the enemy target. This is entered from ACTMENU.
  • DIALOGUERESULT: the battle dialog box is active (usually after using an item or selecting an action).

All STATE branches explained (states with loops at the beginning/end are marked with an asterisk):

No opening attack: 

ACTIONSELECT* -> FIGHTMENU -> ATTACKING -> DEFENDING -> ACTIONSELECT*

With opening attack: 

DEFENDING* -> ACTIONSELECT -> FIGHTMENU -> ATTACKING -> DEFENDING*

Choose an action: 

ACTIONSELECT* -> ACTMENU -> ACTSELECTING -> DIALOGUERESULT -> ACTIONSELECT*

Choose items: 

ACTIONSELECT* -> ITEMMENU -> DIALOGUERESULT -> ACTIONSELECT*

Choose mercy: 

ACTIONSELECT* -> MERCYMENU -> ACTIONSELECT*(/DEFENDING -> ACTIONSELECT*)

docs.title: Game and Enemy System
docs.text: After understanding how the battle scene roughly works, let's learn about the enemy system that is bound to the battle scene. Since the previous topic already introduced where enemy files are located, this time we will open an Encounter.lua file directly.

docs.title: Quick Overview
docs.text:
Opening the file may look messy at first, so let's only extract the useful parts. 

local encounter = {
    EncounterText = localize.EncounterText,
    Gold = 0,
    Exp = 0,
    Enemies = {
        {
            name = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Name,
            defensetext = "MISS",
            misstext = "MISS",
            exp = 1,
            gold = 1,
            maxhp = 100,
            hp = 100,
            maxdamage = 999,

            killable = true,
            canspare = false,
            dead = false,

            actions = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Actions,
            position = {
                x = 320 - 150,
                y = 140
            }
        },
        {
            name = localize.Enemies.TheOther.Name,
            defensetext = "MISS",
            misstext = "MISS",
            exp = 9999,
            gold = 1,
            maxhp = 100,
            hp = 80,
            maxdamage = 109,

            killable = true,
            canspare = false,
            dead = false,

            actions = localize.Enemies.TheOther.Actions,
            position = {
                x = 320,
                y = 140
            }
        }
    },

    Inventory = {
        Pattern = 1,
        NoDelete = false,
        Items = {
            localize.Items.Chocolate,
            localize.Items.End
        }
    },
    STATE = "ACTIONSELECT",
    Player = PlayerLib.Init({
        name = "Chara",
        lv = 1,
        maxhp = 100,
        hp = 100
    }),
    WIN = false
}

return encounter

Leaving aside other details again, let's look at what each enemy table contains.

{
    name = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Name,
    defensetext = "MISS",
    misstext = "MISS",
    exp = 1,
    gold = 1,
    maxhp = 100,
    hp = 100,
    maxdamage = 999,

    killable = true,
    canspare = false,
    dead = false,

    actions = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Actions,
    position = {
        x = 320 - 150,
        y = 140
    }
},

docs.title: Enemy Properties
docs.text:
• name: the enemy's name. This example uses localization.
  • defensetext: text shown when the player swings and deals no damage. You can change this to NO EFFECT using ULB.
  • misstext: text shown when the player enters the aiming screen but does not swing, causing the attack bar to go out of bounds and MISS.
  • exp: how much EXP this enemy gives.
  • gold: how much gold this enemy gives.
  • maxhp: the enemy's maximum HP.
  • hp: the enemy's current HP. In the original UNDERTALE, if you do not spare a monster and leave, the enemy may have less HP on the next fight. You can model this by changing hp.
  • maxdamage: the maximum damage the player can deal. This system does not use complicated atk/def calculations; it directly uses maxdamage. You can also recalculate a new maxdamage every frame to simulate random damage.
  • killable: whether the enemy dies when its HP reaches zero. For example, Napstablook does not immediately end the battle when HP reaches zero; they continue talking before the battle ends. In that case, set killable to false first and then set it to true later.
  • canspare: whether the enemy can be spared.
  • dead: whether the enemy is dead.
  • actions: the actions available from this enemy.
  • position: the target position used when the player swings.

docs.title: Player Settings
docs.text:
• Player: set the player's name, level, max HP, and current HP here.
  • Inventory: the player's item bag. It can hold many items and will automatically lay out the interface. (But in Overworld and chest interactions, the player can only hold up to eight items.)

docs.title: Opening Attack Effect
docs.text:
Now that we know all the important information, let's make an opening attack like Sans.

First, find the STATE field. This determines the initial state when entering a battle.

Since we know that when STATE is DEFENDING, the game immediately enters the enemy turn, we can set STATE to DEFENDING to make the scene start with an opening attack.

STATE = "DEFENDING"

docs.title: Enemy MISS Effect
docs.text:
Although we already know how to set a MISS text (defensetext and misstext), we still need to know how to make an enemy avoid the player's attack.

To do that, set maxdamage to a value less than 0. This makes the attack unable to deal damage and triggers a MISS effect.

{
    name = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Name,
    defensetext = "MISS",
    misstext = "MISS",
    exp = 1,
    gold = 1,
    maxhp = 100,
    hp = 100,
    maxdamage = -999, -- damage becomes -999

    killable = true,
    canspare = false,
    dead = false,

    actions = localize.Enemies.Poseur.Actions,
    position = {
        x = 320 - 150,
        y = 140
    }
},

The enemy above will successfully avoid the attack and show the MISS text.

docs.title: Wave Development
docs.text: As an Undertale fan project, there is always a turn-based system. In this guide, I usually call a turn a wave; you can also call it a turn. They are almost the same in this context.

docs.title: Connection to the Battle Scene
docs.text:
All wave files are located in the Scripts/Waves/ folder. If a battle scene wants a wave, it will look for it there.

In general, we set round progression inside the DefenseEnding function. 

local nextwaves = {"wave_test1", "wave_test2", "wave_test3", "wave_test4"}
b.battle.nextwave = "wave_test1"

local waveProgress = 1

local function DefenseEnding()
    waveProgress = waveProgress + 1
    if (waveProgress > #nextwaves) then waveProgress = 1 end

    b.battle.nextwave = nextwaves[waveProgress]
end

What this does is set up the sequence of waves in nextwaves, then assign the first wave using b.battle.nextwave.

The waveProgress variable tracks the wave progression and can be used to loop the sequence back to the beginning. In most cases, you can simply use b.battle.nextwave = nextwaves[waveProgress] to advance one wave at a time.

docs.title: Quick Overview
docs.text:
Let's quickly look at the most useful parts of a wave file:

local Arena = battle.mainarena
local Player = battle.Player

local wave = {
    ENDED = false,
    objects = {}
}

local function EndWave()
    wave.ENDED = true
    arenas.clear()
    for i = #wave.objects, 1, -1 do
        local obj = wave.objects[i]
        obj:Destroy()
        table.remove(wave.objects, i)
    end
end

Arena:Resize(155, 130)
Player.sprite:MoveTo(320, 320)

local mask = masks.New("rectangle", 320, 320, 155, 130, 0, 1)

function wave.update(dt)
    mask:Follow(Arena.black)

    for i = #wave.objects, 1, -1 do
        local obj = wave.objects[i]
        if (obj.logic) then
            obj:logic(dt)
        end
    end
end

function wave.draw()
end

return wave
  • Arena = battle.mainarena: this is the battle's main arena. Since later fan games can add extra frames and shapes, it is important to distinguish the main battle arena. battle.mainarena is the value for that.
  • Player = battle.Player: the main soul/player object.
  • EndWave(): the most important function. It ends the current wave and returns to the main flow.
  • Arena:Resize(155, 130): resize the battle box to (155, 130) when entering the wave.
  • Player.sprite:MoveTo(320, 320): place the player's soul in the center of the battle box.
  • mask = masks.New(...): see the Mask section for details.

docs.title: Battle Box
docs.text:
All battle boxes use a black-over-white structure: first draw the white rectangle, then cover it with a black rectangle.

Overall, the battle box has many useful methods.

docs.title: Battle Box Methods
docs.text:
• Arena:Resize(w, h): set the target size of the battle box. The box will animate to that size.
  • Arena:MoveTo(x, y): set the target position of the battle box and move it there.
  • Arena:MoveToAndResize(x, y, w, h): move and resize at the same time.
  • Arena:UpSide(value): keep the current center and extend the box upward by value pixels.
  • Arena:DownSide, LeftSide, RightSide: same idea for other sides.
  • RotateTo(rotation): set the target rotation and rotate there.
  • Arena:Destroy(): destroy the battle box (do not use this on the main battle box!).

docs.title: Instantly Changing Battle Box Properties
docs.text:
You may notice that the above methods do not change properties instantly; they animate into the target values.

Fortunately, there is also a way to change properties directly.

Because the battle box has both current properties and target properties, you can manually set the current values after setting the target values.

-- This sets only the target size, and the animation still runs.
Arena:Resize(155, 130)

-- Since the target size is set, you can also set the current size directly.
Arena:Resize(155, 130)
Arena.width = 155
Arena.height = 130

docs.title: Bones
docs.text: Yes, I know you like bones. Now let's create a bone.

docs.title: Creating a 2D Bone
docs.text:
Since there is no direct bone creation function in a wave file, we first need to require the library. 

local bones = require("Scripts.Libraries.Attacks.Bones")
Then we can create a bone with bones:New2D(whose, length):

  • whose: whose bone it is. The valid values are Sans or Papyrus.
  • length: how long the bone is. This length includes the head and tail, so subtract 12 to get the bone shaft length.
-- This bone will exactly fill a battle box width of 130.
local bone = bones:New2D("Sans", 130 - 12)

docs.title: 2D Bone Methods
docs.text:
Some bones only appear inside the arena. They do not extend outside because of masks, so bones support mask settings and other controls:

  • bone:SetStencils(masks): set the bone's masks.
  • bone:Length(length): change the bone's length; it must be >= 0.
  • bone:SetColor(color): set the bone's overall color (not attack type; attack type is set separately).
  • bone:SetMode(mode): set the bone's attack mode, such as orange, blue, normal, etc.
  • bone:ToUp(arena): attach one end of the bone to the top edge of the given arena. Here the arena is the main battle arena.
  • ToDown, ToLeft, ToRight: same idea for other directions.
  • bone:Destroy(): destroy the bone.

docs.title: Creating a 3D Bone Cube
docs.text:
Yes, the above section covers only 2D bones. Now let's show a flashy 3D bone cube.

First, understand that a 3D bone is not created directly as a 3D bone. You create a cube first, then connect the eight points with lines to form a 3D bone cube.

So we start by creating a cube:

local cube = bones:New3D({
    {320 - 100, 240 - 100, 50}, {320 + 100, 240 - 100, 50},
    {320 - 100, 240 + 100, 50}, {320 + 100, 240 + 100, 50},
    {320 - 100, 240 - 100, 250}, {320 + 100, 240 - 100, 250},
    {320 - 100, 240 + 100, 250}, {320 + 100, 240 + 100, 250}
})

I chose the center point as (320, 240, 150). The center does not need to be entered manually; the code automatically calculates the average of these points.

docs.title: Connecting the 3D Bone Cube
docs.text:
Since we created eight points, they are stored in cube. Now use cube:Link(whose, p1, p2) to connect these points, producing 12 edges.

cube:Link("Sans", 1, 2)
cube:Link("Sans", 1, 3)
cube:Link("Sans", 2, 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 3, 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 1 + 4, 2 + 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 1 + 4, 3 + 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 2 + 4, 4 + 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 3 + 4, 4 + 4)
cube:Link("Sans", 1, 5)
cube:Link("Sans", 2, 6)
cube:Link("Sans", 3, 7)
cube:Link("Sans", 4, 8)

Then the 3D bone cube will connect successfully.

If the bones are too long and error tolerance becomes too low, you can use a percentage to reduce the length.

cube.percent = 0.4

This makes all bone lengths 0.4 times their original length.

docs.title: 3D Bone Methods
docs.text:
3D bones also have methods:

  • Link(whose, p1, p2): connect points.
  • Rotate(axis, angle): rotate around x, y, or z.
  • MoveTo(x, y): move the cube.
  • MoveToRelative(x, y): move the cube's center.
  • SetStencils(masks): set masks.
  • Destroy(): destroy the cube.

docs.title: Bone Wall
docs.text:
Use bones:Wall(arena, whose, warntime, staytime, length, direction, rotation, interval, animation) to create a bone wall.

  • arena: which battle arena.
  • whose: whose bone.
  • warntime: warning time.
  • staytime: how long the wall stays after extending.
  • length: how long it extends.
  • direction: one of up, down, left, right.
  • rotation: currently unavailable.
  • interval: currently unavailable.
  • animation: the wall animation with extend/retract timing.

Example: 

local wall = bones:Wall(battle.mainarena, "Papyrus", 40, 30, 50, "down", 10, 13, {
    In = "SineOut", Out = "BackIn",
    It = 15         , Ot  = 60
})
wall:SetStencils({mask})

At present, the bone wall only supports setting masks and has no other methods.

docs.title: GasterBlaster
docs.text:
Use blasters:New(begin, final, angles, waittime, firingtime, sprite_prefix, sounds_prefix) to create a GB cannon.

  • begin: start position.
  • final: end position.
  • angles: format like {0, 90} for start and end angles.
  • waittime: how long to wait before firing (recommended not less than 40).
  • firingtime: how long the firing lasts.
  • sprite_prefix: sprite path prefix.
  • sounds_prefix: sound path prefix.
local gb1 = blasters:New(
    {0, 0}, {320, 240},
    {180, 0},

    40, 10,

    nil,
    nil
)

docs.title: Callback Function
docs.text:
When the GB fires, there is a hidden function that triggers once at that moment.

Continuing from gb1:

gb1.TimeCall = function()
    print("The cannon fired")
end

You will see the message appear in the terminal, so you can control the exact moment of firing.

There is also a function called Beam, which is unique to GB. So you can write:

gb1.TimeCall = function()
    gb1:Beam(30)
end

This fires a laser at a 30° angle when the cannon fires.

You can also disable the default laser and make it a dud cannon.

gb1.default_fire = false

docs.title: Custom Attack Effects
docs.text: Maybe you are tired of the common stick and knife attacks. No problem: SoulEngine supports custom attack styles, and all attack effects are created by you.

docs.title: Attack File Location
docs.text:
As mentioned before, a battle can customize its attack style. Those attack files are located in Scripts/Libraries/Battle/Patterns/.

There are three files inside: - doubleatk: used for double-attack effects. - stick: the classic stick/knife attack. - template: the base template used for copying and modifying.

docs.title: Attack Execution Order
docs.text:
Next, let's explain the underlying logic order of an attack so you can better understand how the system works.

First, the player enters the ATTACKING state. This state triggers the first attack library function, Create(). Here you initialize the battle UI, such as creating an aim target and a moving bar for the original style, or creating a six-shot cylinder for a Clover-style weapon.

Second, after creation, all logic is handled by the Update function. This includes when the bar moves, how it moves, when it stops, how damage is calculated, or whether the player can press confirm multiple times. The attack logic is fully controlled by your Update code.

Finally, the total damage is calculated and passed to finalHurt, which handles the rest of the battle resolution.

Here is the full process:

STATE Attack Animation Behavior Description Entering ATTACKING Call Create Initialize all attack UI and conditions ATTACKING Continuously call Update Custom attack style updates continuously ~ATTACKING (not well defined, usually ATTACKING) BattleInit waits for _end to finish the attack and calculates damage The battle system waits for _end to be set to true, then advances the flow ~(any state except ATTACKING) Call Reset Reset all attack parameters and variables for the next attack

In short, you only need to modify these three parts to create a custom attack animation and effect.

docs.title: Creating an Attack Animation
docs.text: After the above explanation, you should have a general idea of the logic. Now let's write a custom attack animation.

docs.title: Underlying Variables
docs.text:
These variables are mandatory and must be set, because the lower-level system depends on them having definite values.

  • enemy: every attack animation needs this enemy object to get enemy information, such as the target position.
  • _end: if set to true, the lower-level system will immediately process the attack result. Only set this to true after the attack truly ends. For example, in a stick/knife attack, set it after the player presses confirm and the bar stops.
  • finalHurt: set this before _end. If you want the attack to miss, set a negative value.

All other variables are user-defined (refer to the template file for examples).

docs.title: Understanding the Default Attack Template
docs.text:
You may want to know what the default template does before copying it. Let's break it down.

First, ignore the content before Create and only read the Create function: 

function template.Create()
    template._has_sliced = false
    template.finalHurt = 0
    template.Elements = {}

    -- target (example)
    local target = sprites.CreateSprite("UI/Battle Screen/spr_target_0.png", 12)
    target.y = 320
    target:MoveTo(320, target.y)
    table.insert(template.Elements, target)

    -- moving bar (example)
    local bar = sprites.CreateSprite("UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_targetchoice_0.png", 16)
    bar.y = 320
    bar:SetAnimation({
        "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_targetchoice_1.png",
        "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_targetchoice_0.png"
    }, 5)
    -- randomly begin from left or right to stay compatible with the original logic
    local pos = math.random(1, 2)
    if pos == 1 then
        bar.x = 320 + 280
        bar.velocity.x = -6
        bar.newPosvar = 2
    else
        bar.x = 320 - 280
        bar.velocity.x = 6
        bar.newPosvar = 1
    end
    table.insert(template.Elements, bar)
end
Explanation:

It instantly creates a target, which is the aiming area, and then creates a bar, which is the moving attack bar.
