When the bar is created, the pos variable randomly chooses the start direction. If pos == 1, the bar moves in from the right; otherwise, it moves in from the left.

These two sprites are then inserted into template.Elements so Update can control them later.

Now let's look at Update: 

function template.Update(dt)
    -- If there is no enemy (extreme case), return immediately.
    if not template.enemy then return end

    -- If the player presses confirm (this example uses keyboard.GetState, consistent with Battle)
    if keyboard.GetState("confirm") == 1 and not template._has_sliced then
        template._has_sliced = true
        template._end = true
        print("ATTACKED BY LIBRARY")

        -- play sound and create the slice effect at the enemy's position
        audio.PlaySound("snd_slice.wav")
        local slice = sprites.CreateSprite("UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_0.png", 18)
        slice:SetAnimation({
            "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_1.png",
            "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_2.png",
            "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_3.png",
            "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_4.png",
            "UI/Battle Screen/Player Attack/spr_slice_o_5.png"
        }, 10, "oneshot-empty")

        -- use the enemy's current position
        local e = template.enemy
        if e and e.position then
            slice:MoveTo(e.position.x, e.position.y)
        else
            slice:MoveTo(320, 320) -- fallback to center to avoid crashes
        end
        table.insert(template.Elements, slice)

        -- calculate damage (example: based on distance between bar and target)
        local damage = template.GetMaxDamage() or 0
        local tar = template.Elements[1]
        local bar = template.Elements[2]
        bar.velocity.x = 0  -- stop the bar
        if tar and bar and bar.x and tar.x then
            local absLength = math.abs(bar.x - tar.x)
            if absLength > 5 then
                damage = math.ceil(damage * 0.9 * (1 - absLength / 280))
            end
        end

        -- write the final damage back to the pattern. Battle uses this during resolution.
        template.finalHurt = damage
    end

    -- check if the bar went out of bounds; if so, treat it as a miss and set 0 damage.
    local bar = template.Elements[2]
    if bar and (bar.x < 40 or bar.x > 600) and not template._has_sliced then
        template._has_sliced = true
        template._end = true
        template.finalHurt = -999
        bar.velocity.x = 0
    end
end
Explanation:

If the player presses attack and this is the first press (controlled by _has_sliced), the code creates a slice effect and sets _end = true. This tells the battle system that the attack is complete, the player can no longer act, and the system should resolve the attack.

It uses the enemy's position to place the attack effect at the enemy.

Then it stops the bar, calculates the damage based on the bar's position, and stores the result in template.finalHurt, which the battle system uses.

Finally, it handles the case where the player never attacks by checking whether the bar leaves the screen. In that case, it sets _end = true and the attack ends with a miss.

docs.title: Overworld Structure and Location
docs.text: In SoulEngine, the Overworld system is a major component and one of the engine's distinguishing features. First, we need to understand how Overworld is organized.

docs.title: Overworld Scenes
docs.text:
Overworld scenes are not maps; they are the code files that control the scene behind the game.

These scene directories are located in Scripts/Scenes/Overworld/. The engine includes several test scenes there.

  • scene_ow_new: the new Overworld scene, which is the best template to copy.
  • scene_ow_ruins_1: a fallback scene used to test scene transitions.
  • scene_snowball: a snowball scene. A small secret: this scene's kicking behavior is not very accurate because its wall collision calculations are buggy. Still, the player can kick the snowball.

docs.title: Overworld Battle Scene
docs.text:
During Overworld exploration, the player will encounter enemies. The battle scene for Overworld encounters is located at Scripts/Scenes/Battle/scene_battle_ow.

Of course, this is not the only scene. There is also scene_battle_spider in the same directory, which is an older test scene.

Later, you can set up your own encounter scene — it is not locked to just one.

docs.title: Overworld Maps
docs.text:
Now that we know where the scenes are, let's find the maps.

You need to leave the Scripts folder and look at the root directory.

You will find a folder named Maps. This is where all Overworld map files are stored.

I will explain how to use the map in a scene later. For now, just locate the path.

docs.title: Overworld Sprite Resources
docs.text:
Some of you may think the sprites are in Resources/Sprites/, and you are right, but that folder contains many assets. It may not be obvious where to find what you need.

  • Overworld: the most obvious name. This folder contains the large map sprites for original characters, and other action sprites like Sans eating ice cream.
  • Scene: scene sprites, including map tiles. If you want to create an Undertale-style fan game, you can use these tiles. Chest icons, save points, signboards, and other UI sprites are also here.

docs.title: Core Overworld Principles
docs.text:
Maybe you have already written a room and want it to connect to the Overworld, but you don't know where to start.

Now let's learn how Overworld connects to other scenes through its lower-level system.

docs.title: Core Variable Analysis
docs.text:
• Overworld: a global variable. It must be obtained via global:GetSaveVariable and stored as the usable DATA global variable.
  • Flags: a global variable. It must be obtained via global:GetSaveVariable and stored as the usable FLAG global variable.
  • Chests: a global variable. It must be obtained via global:GetSaveVariable and stored as the usable CHESTS global variable.

docs.title: Overworld Variable Contents
docs.text:
• time: global time. It increments continuously while the game is running and not on the save screen.
  • room_name: not the actual room location; it is the room name shown in the save menu.
  • room: the actual room identifier. This must be accurate because the player will return to this room when loading a save.
  • marker: which landmark point is used as the room's first position. This is typically used for room transitions.
  • position: stores the player's location when saving, or the player's current location after encountering enemies. The player returns to this position after the battle ends.
  • direction: stores the player's facing direction at the same time as position.
  • savedpos: whether the current data should be saved when changing rooms. If it is not true, position and direction are cleared.
  • player: a player table that contains player attributes.
    • name: player name.
    • lv: player level.
    • maxhp: player maximum HP.
    • hp: player current HP.
    • gold: player's gold.
    • exp: player's experience.
    • atk: base attack from leveling.
    • watk: weapon attack bonus.
    • def: base defense from leveling.
    • edef: armor defense bonus.
    • weapon: the weapon, default is Stick.
    • armor: the armor, default is Bandage.
    • items: the default items in the bag, which are also needed for battle.
    • getcell: whether the phone has been obtained (unlocks the phone menu).
    • cells: which options are available.

docs.title: Flags Variable Contents
docs.text:
Note: these are the default flags. You can add new variables freely.

  • ruins_killed: how many enemies have been killed in the ruins. Each kill increments this value via the OVERWORLD_KILLFLAG mechanism.
  • ruins_0: a testing variable used for the triangle rune interaction. It simulates a chest that can be opened only once. Once the item is taken, the chest stays empty.
    • donut_chest: whether the donut has been taken from the chest (this item never appears because I removed the code, but you can still find it in the item library).
  • text_inst: a test trigger for story events. If you pass through the trigger and it activates a story event, it will not trigger again unless the story is designed to repeat. Normally, Toriel will never say the same thing twice.

docs.title: Chests Variable Contents
docs.text:
This stores the contents of multiple chests, which is useful for dimension-shifting chest designs.

  • chest1: the first chest, which is the default chest in use.
  • chest2: a second test chest. You can use it later for the phone system or other interactions.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: If you are not sure what the wrench icon means, go to the homepage and review the description of the icon first.

docs.title: Install the Mapping Tool
docs.text: The tool used in this guide is Tiled. It is a convenient and stable map editor, which is why I recommend it.

docs.title: Download
docs.text:
On the website, click the circled Download on itch.io button to go to the itch.io page and download Tiled.

If the page does not load or is difficult to access, you can use this alternative link:

https://wwatg.lanzouq.com/ipNqa3iggbmh
password: tiled
Open link

After extracting the archive, double-click the .msi file to install.

docs.title: After downloading, open Tiled. You will see a large blank page with only a few buttons. You are ready for the next topic.
Create and Draw a Map
docs.text: Required tool: Tiled.

docs.title: Initialize the Map
docs.text: After opening Tiled, you will likely only see a few buttons. Find and click the Create New Map button.

docs.title: Set Basic Tile Properties
docs.text: After clicking Create New Map, a window appears asking you to set tile properties. Set them exactly as shown in the image below. Then click OK to continue.

docs.title: Save the Map First
docs.text:
Once the map is created, go to the top-left File menu and click Save.

Make sure the save path begins from your project root at Maps/tmx/! I will explain why this matters later.

If you see other .tmx files in the folder, the save location is correct. Rename the file and save it.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: At this point, the Tiled map project is created.

docs.title: Draw the Map
docs.text: Before continuing, if you do not know what a tile is, read the first section to understand it, then continue map creation.

docs.title: What is a Tile?
docs.text:
Imagine you have a huge world map (for example, 10 meters wide and tall) and want to hang it on the wall. The map is too large to carry.

The solution is to cut the map into many small 1-meter squares. Each small piece is a tile. You can place one tile at a time, and when all tiles are assembled, the full world map is restored.

Tile maps have three characteristics:

  • Tiling: the map is divided into many identical square pieces (often 256x256 or 512x512 pixels).
  • Layers: the map has different levels, such as a world layer, continent layer, and street layer. Higher levels contain more tiles and more detail.
  • Indexing: each tile has a unique identifier, usually based on x, y, and z coordinates, so the computer can find and load it quickly.

Now that you know what tiles are and how they work, let's continue.

docs.title: Import a Tileset
docs.text:
Click File -> New -> New Tileset to create a tileset.

Then set the tile size to 20x20 pixels to match our map. Also check Embed in map. If you do not embed the tileset, the map may fail to load in the game.

Next, click Browse... in the Image box. Note: the settings below are critical.

When the browser opens, if it starts in Maps/tmx, then the location is correct. Now select a tileset.

Then go back one directory and open the images folder. Select the ruins tileset (or any other image you want to use, as long as you paste tiles from it). Click Open to import the tileset.

docs.title: Select a Tile and Paint
docs.text:
In the lower-right corner, click the Tileset panel. This is where you choose which tile to paint with.

Click a tile to select it, then drag on the map editor to paint with it.

docs.title: Add a New Layer
docs.text:
Suppose you have a vine tile and a wall tile. If you draw them on the same layer, they cannot coexist: either the wall appears and the vine disappears, or the vine appears and the wall disappears.

In this case, add a new layer and draw them separately in the same cells.

First click Layers in the lower-right corner to switch to edit layer mode. Then click the first icon — a sheet of paper with a yellow star — to add a Tile Layer. This solves the drawing conflict described above.

docs.title: Draw Animated Tiles
docs.text:
Have you ever wondered how the water under the ruins bridge is animated? No extra code is needed; Tiled can do it.

This works because Tiled has a built-in animation editor.

Open the .tsx file for the tileset in Tiled, then select a tile and click the camera icon to open the tile animation editor.

Note: no matter what millisecond value you choose, the STI library uses a second-based timer. Just make sure the animation feels correct.

docs.title: Save the Map
docs.text: Press Ctrl + S or use File -> Save in the top-left corner to prevent data loss.

docs.title: Do Not Export the Map Yet
docs.text: Although the map is drawn, it is not ready to be used in the game yet. After we explain object layers, then you can export the map for the game. Please read on patiently. Exporting too early will lead to problems.

docs.title: Use Terrain Tiles to Draw Maps Quickly
docs.text:
Suppose you want to create an image like this:

You might need to select many tiles and draw slowly, which easily causes misaligned textures. Or you might spend a lot of effort changing tiles at every corner just to fix a small detail.

Terrain tiles solve this problem.

docs.title: Create a New Terrain Set
docs.text:
To create a new terrain set, click the little wrench icon labeled Edit Terrain Tiles in the lower-right corner.

This opens the terrain editor. The right side is where you create new terrain sets.

docs.title: Choose a Set Type
docs.text:
After entering the terrain editor, click the plus icon in the lower-right corner to create a new terrain set. There are three types, and their names and uses are as follows:

  • Corner Set: suitable for roads, ceilings, and other multi-directional transitions. (A full set contains 16 complete tiles.)
  • Edge Set: suitable for pipes or other tiles that extend in only four directions. (A full set contains 47 complete tiles.)
  • Mixed Set: contains both of the above. (A full set contains 256 tiles.)

If you want to tile terrain, use a Corner Set. If you want to tile artificial pipes or narrow paths that extend in four directions, use an Edge Set.

Here, we choose the large black-bordered option in the upper-right corner to create a Corner Set. This set is best for beginners learning about Corner Sets.

docs.title: Create and Name the Terrain
docs.text: After selecting Corner Set, the upper-right area shows Unnamed Set. Give it a name. Since the name is only displayed in the editor, call it roof. Also change the numeric ID below; I set it to 1.

docs.title: Paint Terrain Areas
docs.text:
After creating the terrain set, hover the mouse over the tiles. You will see a small red dot in the corner. That indicates the terrain area.

In Tiled, the terrain set editor defines the rules for "Auto-Tiles". The core idea is: by selecting a tile's position in the terrain set, you define how it should connect to other terrain on the actual map.

Now let's practice. First, look at the components of a complete tile set.

docs.title: Corner Set Terrain Types
docs.text:
The four red-boxed areas in the image represent:

  • Corner tiles: the outermost corner pieces, used for convex corners.
  • Edge tiles: the outer transition tiles.
  • Inner tiles: the tiles that fill the interior area.
  • Inner corner tiles: the tiles used for concave corners.

Now we can assign roles to each tile region.

docs.title: Assign Each Tile Type
docs.text:
First, inner tiles are the easiest to recognize, so select them and fill the grid.

Next, paint the eight tiles around the inner tile on the side closest to the inner tile.

This means those eight tiles will connect directly to the central tile.

Then the four inner corner tiles remain. These tiles cannot touch the central tile at the corner; only two edges can connect.

So when setting those tiles, avoid the corner that cannot touch the inner tile and fill the other areas.

At this point, the corner set is complete and can be used.

docs.title: Use the Terrain Brush to Draw Maps
docs.text:
Return to the map. Click the terrain brush at the top, then click roof in the right-hand menu and select the terrain ID 1. When you move the mouse over the map, you will see a group of tiles instead of a single tile. You can drag freely, and the shape updates automatically.

Now you can draw terrain freely without having to manually adjust every tile.

docs.title: Adding Objects to the Map
docs.text: If you followed the first topic, then by the end of this topic the map you made in Tiled can be used in the game.

docs.title: Object Concept
docs.text:
You may wonder how a map made in Tiled becomes playable. You may ask:

  • How is the player placed on the map?
  • How can the player interact with objects? (Even walls can block the player.)

Objects are just an interface. The real implementation happens later. For now, we only need to understand how to create objects.

docs.title: Create an Object Layer
docs.text: Objects are not placed on the same tile layers introduced earlier. They are placed on an object layer. First, open the layer view by clicking Layers in the lower right corner, then create an Object Layer to place objects.

docs.title: Object Layer Naming Rules
docs.text:
In SoulEngine, object layers are not named arbitrarily. There are rules. Because the engine needs to manage all Overworld objects precisely, the names must be correct. The naming rules and meanings are:

  • marks: landmark points. The player appears at these points the first time they enter the room. A room may have more than one landmark point. For example, if you enter a corridor with exits to different rooms, the scene may have multiple initial player positions.
  • warps: teleport areas. When the player steps on them, they move to another room's landmark point.
  • walls: wall collision areas. When the player hits them, they are blocked outside the wall.
  • saves: save point locations. When the player steps on them and presses confirm, they can interact and save.
  • signs: sign locations. When the player steps on them and presses confirm, a dialogue appears. For NPC dialogue, I do not recommend using this; use triggers instead.
  • chests: chest locations. When the player steps on them and presses confirm, they open and allow item storage/retrieval. You can also use them like the triangle rune chest, where items can be taken once and then the chest becomes empty.
  • triggers: triggers. This is the most powerful feature. It is equivalent to drawing a region for everything you want to control on the map. Use it for switches, pressure plates, story triggers, hidden areas, and even NPC interactions.

When configured correctly, it should look like this:

Once that is done, you can start adding objects to the object layer.

docs.title: Add Objects to a Specific Layer
docs.text:
First, click one of the object layers. You will see some toolbar options become enabled.

In normal development, we usually use the first tool (select an object), the third tool (draw a rectangle object), and the fourth tool (draw a point object).

docs.title: Shape Type for Each Layer
docs.text:
You might think a save point should use a rectangle, since it has a small area that blocks the player. But actually, save points use points.

To avoid confusion, here are the required object shapes by layer type:

  • marks: use Point.
  • warps: use Rectangle.
  • walls: use Rectangle.
  • saves: use Point.
  • signs: use Point.
  • chests: use Point.
  • triggers: use Rectangle or Point.

docs.title: Add a Property to an Object
docs.text:
Think about it: a landmark point not only records the player's initial position, it also needs to record the player's facing direction.

But if you just place a landmark point, where do you set the player's direction? The answer is: properties.

Select a landmark point (first click the marks layer, then select an object). You will see a blank Custom Properties area on the left. Here you can add the properties you want for that object.

Next, add a variable named direction with type string, then click OK. This creates the direction property successfully. Then add a variable named id with type int.

Here are the object properties and types for each layer:

  • marks

    • direction: player facing direction, type string, with values up, down, left, or right.

  • other

    • any property is allowed.

You may be surprised by this, but the truth is: Only marks requires direction. The other layers have no enforced requirements.

Note: Keep good habits. When creating a new object, give it an id property of type int with a unique value. This will be very helpful later.

docs.title: Auto-align to Grid
docs.text:
Imagine you are drawing walls to limit player movement, but you find precise alignment difficult.

At that point, you may wish the objects would snap to the grid automatically.

Tiled supports this. Hold Ctrl while clicking or dragging the mouse, and the object will snap precisely to the grid. The example below shows the left dashed box drawn without Ctrl, and the right dashed box drawn with Ctrl. The difference is obvious.

docs.title: Exporting Maps
docs.text: After the previous topics, you now have enough knowledge to export a usable map.

docs.title: Export Process
docs.text:
Press Ctrl + Shift + E, or click File -> Export As, to open the export dialog. Choose the .lua format, give the file a name, and save it alongside your other .lua files. (You can also create a dedicated folder for organization; that is not shown here.)

At this point, the map has been successfully exported to the Maps/ folder. If you are ready to use it, move on to the next topic.

docs.title: Updating a Map
docs.text: If the exported map is not quite right, do not worry. This is normal. Make the changes you want, then repeat the export process described above.

docs.title: Using Maps
docs.text:
Quick note 2.5.1: ow.SetBattleScene now accepts a second argument for the flag.

After you export a map, the most important step is using it. Let's create an Overworld scene and use the map.

docs.title: Initialize the Scene
docs.text:
First, prepare a scene file. I recommend copying the code below exactly as shown.

Copy and paste: 

-- This is a template for creating a new scene in the game.
-- You can use this as a starting point for your own scenes.
local SCENE = {}

--_CAMERA_:setBounds(280 - 320, 300 - 240, 280 + 320, 300 + 240)
local ow = require("Scripts.Libraries.Overworld.InitWorld")
ow.InitMusic("mus_gameover.ogg")
ow.Init("Maps/ruins_1.lua", "scene_ow_ruins_1")
ow.DEBUG = true
ow.ENCOUNTER = true
ow.InitEncounter(FLAG.ruins_killed, 80, 40, 3)
ow.SetBattleScene("Battle/scene_battle_ow", "ruins_killed")

-- This is a fake scene for testing purposes.
function SCENE.load()
    -- Load any resources needed for this scene here.
    -- For example, you might load images, sounds, etc.
end

-- This function is called to update the scene.
function SCENE.update(dt)
    -- Update any game logic for this scene here.
    -- For example, you might update animations, handle input, etc.
    ow.Update(dt)

    if (ow.NEXTSTATE ~= nil) then
        ow.CSTATE = ow.NEXTSTATE
        ow.NEXTSTATE = nil
    end
end

-- This function is called to draw the scene.
-- It is called after the main game loop has finished updating.
function SCENE.draw()
    -- Draw the scene here.
    -- For example, you might draw images, text, etc.
    ow.Draw()
end

-- This function is called when the scene is switched away from.
function SCENE.clear()
    -- Clear any resources used by this scene here.
    -- For example, you might unload images, sounds, etc.
    package.loaded["Scripts.Libraries.Overworld.InitWorld"] = nil

    layers.clear()
    ow.Destroy()
end

-- Don't touch this(just one line).
return SCENE

Let's look at what is happening here. First ignore everything after load and focus on initialization: 

--_CAMERA_:setBounds(280 - 320, 300 - 240, 280 + 320, 300 + 240)
local ow = require("Scripts.Libraries.Overworld.InitWorld")
ow.InitMusic("mus_gameover.ogg")
ow.Init("Maps/ruins_1.lua", "scene_ow_ruins_1")
ow.DEBUG = true
ow.ENCOUNTER = true
ow.InitEncounter(FLAG.ruins_killed, 80, 40, 3)

  • ow = require: loads the Overworld initialization library. Without this, Overworld cannot start.
  • ow.InitMusic("mus_gameover.ogg"): sets the room music to mus_gameover.ogg.
  • ow.Init("Maps/ruins_1.lua", "scene_ow_ruins_1"): loads the Maps/ruins_1.lua map and assigns the scene name scene_ow_ruins_1.
  • ow.DEBUG = true: enables debug mode.
  • ow.ENCOUNTER = true: enables random encounters while the player walks in the map.
  • ow.InitEncounter(FLAG.ruins_killed, 80, 40, 3): sets the encounter system. Enemy spawn count is controlled by FLAG.ruins_killed, starts at 80, has a random range of 40, and spawns 3 encounters.

It is clear that to use your own map, you just need to change the ow.Init call.

docs.title: Player Interaction Objects
docs.text:
Use ow.getInteractResult(type, id) to get the interaction result between the player and an object.

  • type: the object type, such as trigger, chest, save, etc. Use the layer name without the plural form.
  • id: the object's ID, which is set in Tiled to identify the object.

For example, ow.getInteractResult("trigger", 1) checks whether the player collides with the trigger object with ID 1.

Example: 

if (ow.getInteractResult("trigger", 1)) then
    if (keyboard.GetState("confirm") == 1) then
        print("You triggered smth.")
    end
end
This means if the player touches trigger ID 1 and presses confirm, something happens.

docs.title: Set Encounter Info
docs.text:
Use ow.InitEncounter(flag, start, range, amount) to initialize the encounter system.

  • flag: the flag that tracks how many enemies have been defeated (usually a value in FLAG).
  • start: minimum encounter time. The player must walk at least this long before encountering enemies.
  • range: random range. The encounter time is start + random(0, range).
  • amount: the total number of enemies to encounter. The system automatically adjusts the encounter time based on the number of kills. As fewer enemies remain, the time factor increases, but it never exceeds 8x to prevent excessively long play.

If you are curious about the original formula, here is the description: 

First define encounter density coefficient ρ:
ρ = min(8, amount / (amount − killed))

As the remaining enemy count (amount - killed) decreases, ρ increases, but it is capped at 8.

Target time threshold:
t = ρ*(base + U(0, range))

U(0, range) is a uniform random number in [0, range].
Recalculate this value after each battle.

docs.title: Set the Encounter Scene
docs.text:
Use ow.SetBattleScene(scene, flag) to set the battle scene used for encounters.

ow.SetBattleScene("Battle/scene_battle_ow", "ruins_killed")
This means when the player encounters an enemy, they go to Battle/scene_battle_ow, and the encounter count is tracked by FLAG.ruins_killed.

docs.title: Create a Dialogue
docs.text:
Use ow.dialogNew(texts, position) to show a dialogue box with the specified text.

The position parameter controls whether the box appears above or below. If omitted, the system chooses a suitable position.

ow.dialogNew({"* Nothing useful here."})

docs.title: Create a Chest Interaction
docs.text:
Use ow.ChestInteract(chest) to interact with a chest.

The chest parameter is the chest key, such as: 

ow.ChestInteract("chest1")
This opens CHESTS.chest1 for the player.

docs.title: Create a Save Interaction
docs.text:
Use ow.SaveInteract(texts, room, position, direction) to create a save interaction.

  • texts: the text shown when the player saves.
  • room: the room name displayed in the save menu.
  • position: the player's position when the save loads.
  • direction: the player's facing direction when the save loads.

ow.SaveInteract(
    {
        "[colorHEX:99ffff]* BAKABAKA",
    },
    "Idk where", {121, -455}, "down"
)
When the player activates the save point, the text BAKABAKA is displayed. The save room name becomes Idk where, the position becomes {121, -455}, and the facing direction becomes down.

docs.title: Change Rooms
docs.text:
Use ow.ChangeScene(scene, mark) to switch the player to another room at a specific landmark.

ow.ChangeScene("Overworld/scene_ow_ruins_1", 1)

docs.title: Localization and Internationalization
docs.text: In the root of SoulEngine, there is a Localization folder containing translation files for two languages and a translation library.

docs.title: Create your own language file
docs.text:
Copy or refer to the variables defined in the other completed files. These variables are reliable. Then open the game and test the translation while playing.

-- Chinese version:
return {
    EncounterText = "*[pattern:chinese] ​你​什么​都​没​遭遇​到​！",
    Enemies = {
        Poseur = {
            Name = "[preset=chinese]​颇似​儿​",
            Actions = {
                "[preset=chd]​查看​", "[preset=chd]​欣赏​"
            }
        },
        TheOther = {
            Name = "[preset=chinese]​敌人​名称​",
            Actions = {
                "[preset=chd]​查看​", "[preset=chd]​欣赏​"
            }
        },
    },
}

-- English version:
return {
    EncounterText = "* You encountered nothing!",
    Enemies = {
        Poseur = {
            Name = "Poseur",
            Actions = {
                "Check", "Appreciate"
            }
        },
        TheOther = {
            Name = "EnemyName",
            Actions = {
                "Check", "Appreciate"
            }
        },
    },
}
In short, all of the variables are correct.

docs.title: What the creator needs to know
docs.text:
The previous section is mostly about the translator's work. Below is what you, as a game creator, need to set up at the engine level:

Use localizetext(language, element, args) to produce localized text, as shown in the example above.

localizetext("en", {"Enemies", "TheOther", "Name"})

This lets you retrieve an enemy name.

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
Sometimes the player's name cannot be known in advance, such as on the death screen:

GameoverText = {
    "[voice:v_fluffybuns.wav][speed:0.5]* %s!\n* [pattern:chinese]​保持​你​的​决心​.",
}

If the name cannot be determined, you need a placeholder to fill in the player's name. Then use:

localizetext("en", "GameoverText", {Player.name})

This fills the %s placeholder with the player's name.

docs.title: First impression: What is Lua?
docs.text:
You can think of Lua as a very obedient, but somewhat "rigid", calculator assistant. You give it commands, and it executes them one by one. Its core work is two things:

  1. Remember things (store data)
  2. Do calculations and operations (execute commands)

Now let's see how to give instructions to this "little assistant".

docs.title: Part One: Making Lua "remember things" (variables)
docs.text:
To have Lua remember something, we use "variables." Variables are like labeled boxes.

Code demonstration:

-- Let me remember your name
myName = "Xiaoming"

-- Let me remember your age
myAge = 18

-- Let me remember fruit prices
applePrice = 5.5  -- Apple costs 5.5 per jin
bananaPrice = 3.2 -- Banana costs 3.2 per jin

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• myName, myAge, applePrice are the "labels" (variable names).
  • = "Xiaoming", = 18 are the contents placed in the boxes (data).
  • = is not "equal" in math here; it means "assign" — put the right side into the left variable.

  • -- starts a comment for humans; Lua ignores it.

  • Data has different types:

    • "Xiaoming" in quotes is text (a string).
    • 18 without a decimal point is an integer (a number).
    • 5.5 with a decimal point is a decimal number (a number).

docs.title: Part Two: Let Lua "do operations and calculations"
docs.text:
Remembering things isn't enough; we need Lua to work. The most basic work is arithmetic and text concatenation.

Code demonstration:

-- The values we remembered earlier
myName = "Xiaoming"
applePrice = 5.5
bananaPrice = 3.2

-- 1. Math: How much does 2 jin of apples and 3 jin of bananas cost?
totalCost = applePrice * 2 + bananaPrice * 3

-- 2. Text concatenation: Create a greeting
greeting = "Hello, " .. myName .. "!"

-- 3. Make it "speak" (print to the screen)
print("Total cost is: " .. totalCost .. " yuan")
print(greeting)

When you run this, you will see:

Total cost is: 21.4 yuan
Hello, Xiaoming!

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• +, -, *, / are addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
  • .. is two English dots used to join two pieces of text.
  • print() is one of the most important commands; it means "show the content inside the parentheses on the screen." It is our window for seeing Lua's results.

docs.title: Part Three: Let Lua make choices
docs.text:
In real life we often need to make choices, such as "If it rains, I take an umbrella." Lua can make decisions too.

Code demonstration:

-- Remember how much money I have
myMoney = 50
-- Remember the price of a milk tea
milkTeaPrice = 20

print("I have " .. myMoney .. " yuan")
print("A cup of milk tea costs " .. milkTeaPrice .. " yuan")

-- If...then...
if myMoney >= milkTeaPrice then
    -- If the condition is true (my money >= milk tea price), execute this block
    print("Great, I can buy a milk tea!")
    myMoney = myMoney - milkTeaPrice -- After buying the milk tea, the money decreases
    print("After buying milk tea, I have " .. myMoney .. " yuan left")
else
    -- Otherwise (if the money is not enough), execute this block
    print("Oops, not enough money, I can't afford milk tea.")
end

print("Decision finished, life goes on~")

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• if condition then starts the decision. The condition is a question, such as "is my money greater than or equal to the milk tea price?".
  • If the answer is "yes" (the condition is true), Lua executes the commands between then and else.
  • If the answer is "no" (the condition is false), Lua skips that part and executes the commands between else and end.
  • end indicates the end of the conditional block.

docs.title: Part Four: Let Lua repeat work
docs.text:
If you want Lua to count from 1 to 10, you don't need to write print ten times. You can use a loop.

Code demonstration:

-- Make Lua repeat something (for...do...)
print("I am starting to count:")
for i = 1, 10 do
    print("This is time " .. i)
end
print("Done counting!")

-- Another example: calculate 1+2+3+...+10
sum = 0
for number = 1, 10 do
    sum = sum + number -- Add each number to sum
    print("Now adding " .. number .. ", total is " .. sum)
end
print("The final total is: " .. sum)

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• for i = 1, 10 do means: let the variable i start at 1 and end at 10, repeating the commands between do and end.
  • Each time it runs, i increases by 1 (first 1, then 2, ... until 10).

docs.title: Part Five: Let Lua "remember a process" (functions)
docs.text:
Imagine every morning you ask the assistant to do three things:

  • make you a cup of coffee
  • tell you today's weather
  • remind you of today's meeting

You don't need to repeat these commands every morning. You can pack them into a "morning routine." Then each morning you just say "execute the morning routine," and the assistant will do these three things automatically.

This packed process is a function in Lua.

Code demonstration:

-- 1. Define a function (pack a process)
-- The function name is `morningRoutine`
function morningRoutine()
    print("1. Your coffee has been made.")
    print("2. Today's weather is sunny to cloudy, 15-25°C.")
    print("3. There is a project meeting at 10:00 AM. Please be on time.")
    print("--- Morning briefing complete ---")
end

-- After defining it, Lua only remembers the routine; it does not execute it yet.

print("Now it is 9:00 AM")
print("Shall we start the morning routine?")

-- 2. Call the function (execute the packed process)
-- Just write the function name followed by parentheses
morningRoutine()

print("Alright, let's start working!")

docs.title: Running result
docs.text:
Now it is 9:00 AM
Shall we start the morning routine?
1. Your coffee has been made.
2. Today's weather is sunny to cloudy, 15-25°C.
3. There is a project meeting at 10:00 AM. Please be on time.
--- Morning briefing complete ---
Alright, let's start working!

docs.title: One power of functions
docs.text:
They can accept different input and produce different results (functions with parameters).

If you want your coffee sometimes with sugar and sometimes without, you can make the function accept an "instruction."

-- Define a function that accepts an instruction
function makeCoffee(instruction)
    print("Making your coffee..." .. instruction)
end

-- Call the function with different input
makeCoffee(" with sugar")    -- Output: Making your coffee... with sugar
makeCoffee(" with milk")     -- Output: Making your coffee... with milk
makeCoffee(" plain")         -- Output: Making your coffee... plain

docs.title: Another power of functions
docs.text:
They can give you a result (functions with return values).

Let the function do a calculation and then give the result back to you.

-- Define a function to calculate the area of a rectangle
-- It needs you to tell it the length and width
function calculateArea(length, width)
    local area = length * width  -- compute the area inside the function
    return area  -- use the `return` keyword to give the result back
end

-- Call the function and store its return value
myRoomArea = calculateArea(5, 4)  -- calculate the area of a 5 by 4 room
print("My room area is: " .. myRoomArea .. " square meters") -- Output: My room area is: 20 square meters

-- You can also use the function result directly
print("The table area is: " .. calculateArea(2, 1) .. " square meters") -- Output: The table area is: 2 square meters

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Defining a function is like creating a vending machine: you set up its internal workflow (function...end).
  • Parameters are like inserting coins and choosing products.
  • Return values are the goods the machine gives back.
  • Calling a function is like using the vending machine: you give a simple command and the complex work happens inside.

docs.title: Part Six: Let Lua "manage a group of related information" (Tables)
docs.text:
So far, our variables have been independent boxes. But in the real world, many things come in groups, such as:

  • a student's information: name, student ID, grades.
  • all of the items in a shopping cart.
  • all the days of the week.

If you create a separate variable for each item, things get messy. Lua uses tables to solve this problem. Tables are the only data structure in Lua, and they are super important!

You can think of a table as a big folder or a storage cabinet with many compartments.

Code demonstration:

  1. Table as a "folder" (store descriptive information)
-- Create a table to store student Xiaoming's profile
-- Use curly braces {} to create a table
studentXiaoMing = {
    name = "Xiaoming",   -- name is the attribute name, "Xiaoming" is the value
    studentId = 1001,
    mathScore = 90,
    englishScore = 85
}

-- How do we view the contents of the folder?
-- Use folderName.attribute or folderName["attribute"]
print("Student name: " .. studentXiaoMing.name) -- method one (more common)
print("Student ID: " .. studentXiaoMing["studentId"]) -- method two
print("Math score: " .. studentXiaoMing.mathScore)

-- You can also modify or add information
studentXiaoMing.mathScore = 95 -- modify the math score
studentXiaoMing.class = "Grade 3 Class 2" -- add class information

print("After the change, Xiaoming's math score is: " .. studentXiaoMing.mathScore)
print("Xiaoming's class is: " .. studentXiaoMing.class)
  1. Table as a "storage cabinet" (store a list of similar items)
-- Create a to-do list table
-- This kind of table numbers each slot in order (starting from 1)
todoList = {"eat breakfast", "write code", "rest", "continue coding", "sleep"}

-- How do we see what is stored in each slot?
-- Use tableName[index]
print("The first item is: " .. todoList[1]) -- Output: The first item is: eat breakfast
print("The third item is: " .. todoList[3]) -- Output: The third item is: rest

-- If you know the length, you can use a loop to handle the whole table
print("Today's to-do list:")
for i = 1, #todoList do -- #todoList automatically gets the table length
    print(i .. ". " .. todoList[i])
end

A powerful example combining tables and functions:

-- Define a student table
student = {
    name = "Xiaohong",
    scores = {85, 92, 78} -- a table can even contain another table (a score list)
}

-- Define a function to calculate the average score
function calculateAverage(scoreTable)
    local sum = 0
    for i = 1, #scoreTable do
        sum = sum + scoreTable[i]
    end
    return sum / #scoreTable
end

-- Pass Xiaohong's score list to the function to calculate the average
avg = calculateAverage(student.scores)
print(student.name .. "'s average score is: " .. avg)

Output:

Xiaohong's average score is: 85

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Now your "Lua assistant" has been upgraded!

  • Variables: single labeled boxes that store scattered information.
  • Functions: packaged standard workflows. Define once, use many times, accept input and return results. They make code tidy and efficient.
  • Tables: folders or storage cabinets. Use them to store a group of related information neatly. Tables are the foundation for managing complex data in Lua.

docs.title: Part Seven: Let the folder "do things itself" (table methods)
docs.text:
Earlier we compared functions to packed workflows and tables to folders. Now we combine them.

Imagine you have an employee folder that stores employee information:

  • Name: Zhang San
  • Department: Sales
  • Base salary: 5000

If you want to know Zhang San's total salary this month (base salary + sales commission), the old way would be:

  • take the base salary from the folder
  • find an external "calculate total salary" process
  • give the base salary and commission information to that process

That is inconvenient! Is there a smarter way?

Yes. We can put the "calculate salary" process directly into the employee folder. Then the folder becomes "alive" and can calculate its own salary.

Code demonstration:

Method One: create an empty folder first, then put the process into it 

-- 1. Create an empty employee folder
employee = {}

-- 2. Put basic info into the folder
employee.name = "Zhang San"
employee.department = "Sales"
employee.baseSalary = 5000
employee.sales = 80000  -- monthly sales
employee.commissionRate = 0.1 -- commission rate 10%

-- 3. The most important step: put a "calculate total salary" process into the folder
-- Note the syntax: use = to assign the function
employee.calculateTotalSalary = function()
    -- inside the function, you can access other info with employee.xxx
    local commission = employee.sales * employee.commissionRate
    local total = employee.baseSalary + commission
    return total
end

-- Use it: let the folder calculate its own salary
-- Syntax: folderName.functionName()
print(employee.name .. "'s total salary this month is: " .. employee.calculateTotalSalary() .. " yuan")
Method Two: create the folder with the process already inside it (more common) 
-- Create a complete folder at once, containing both data and behavior
employee = {
    name = "Zhang San",
    department = "Sales",
    baseSalary = 5000,
    sales = 80000,
    commissionRate = 0.1,

    -- Define the function directly inside the table
    calculateTotalSalary = function()
        local commission = employee.sales * employee.commissionRate
        local total = employee.baseSalary + commission
        return total
    end
}

-- Usage is exactly the same
print(employee.name .. "'s total salary this month is: " .. employee.calculateTotalSalary() .. " yuan")
Method Three: a more professional way (use self)

The previous code has a small problem: the function hardcodes the name employee. If we rename this folder to staff, the function will break.

A more professional way is to use the special keyword self:

employee = {
    name = "Zhang San",
    baseSalary = 5000,
    sales = 80000,
    commissionRate = 0.1,

    -- Use colon syntax to define the function; Lua automatically provides a `self` parameter
    calculateTotalSalary = function(self)
        -- `self` represents the table that called this function
        local commission = self.sales * self.commissionRate
        local total = self.baseSalary + commission
        return total
    end
}

-- Call using a colon so Lua automatically passes employee as self
print(employee.name .. "'s salary is: " .. employee:calculateTotalSalary() .. " yuan")

The self analogy:

self is like the folder saying "me" to itself. When you say "Folder, calculate your salary," the function inside knows that "me" refers to the folder itself. That way, no matter whether the folder is named employee, staff, or zhangsan, the function uses self to refer to itself and will not break.

A more vivid example: game characters 

-- Create a game character folder
hero = {
    name = "Hero",
    level = 1,
    health = 100,
    attack = 15,

    -- Character's own ability: attack an enemy
    attackEnemy = function(self, enemy)
        print(self.name .. " attacked " .. enemy.name .. "!")
        enemy.health = enemy.health - self.attack
        print(enemy.name .. " has " .. enemy.health .. " health left")
    end,

    -- Character's own ability: level up
    levelUp = function(self)
        self.level = self.level + 1
        self.attack = self.attack + 5
        self.health = self.health + 20
        print(self.name .. " leveled up! Now level " .. self.level .. "!")
    end
}

-- Create a monster folder
monster = {
    name = "Slime",
    health = 30,
    attack = 5
}

-- Let the character act
print("=== Battle Start ===")
hero:attackEnemy(monster)  -- Hero attacks Slime
hero:levelUp()             -- Hero levels up
hero:attackEnemy(monster)  -- Hero attacks Slime again
Running result:

=== Battle Start ===
Hero attacked Slime!
Slime has 15 health left
Hero leveled up! Now level 2!
Hero attacked Slime!
Slime has 0 health left

docs.title: Core idea summary
docs.text:
Table methods = let the folder have its own behavior

Plain variables: the folder's static information (name, age, salary...)

Table methods: the folder's dynamic abilities (calculate salary, attack enemies, level up...)

​这样​设计​的​好处​：

  • ​高度​组织化​：​相关​的​数据​和​操作​都​放在​一起​，​管理​起来​非常​清晰​。
  • ​自成一体​：​档案袋​变得​“​聪明​”​了​，​它​能​自己​处理​自己​的​数据​。
  • ​代码​复用​：​我​可以​创建​很多​个​员工​档案袋​，​每个​都​有​自己​的​ calculateTotalSalary​函数​。

​这​就是​面向对象编程​的​雏形​——​把​数据​和​操作​数据​的​方法​捆绑​在​一起​。​在​ Lua ​中​，​就是​通过​“​表​包含​函数​”​这种​简单​而​强大​的​方式​实现​的​。

​现在​你​再​看​ a = {b = function} ... end​这样​的​代码​，​就​能​明白​：

  • a​是​一个​档案袋​
  • b​是​这个​档案袋​里​的​一个​“​特殊​能力​”
  • ​你​可以​用​ a:b()​来​让​档案袋​ a​执行​它​自己​的​ b​能力​

docs.title: Packaging and Releasing
docs.text:
After finishing your game, the next step is to let others play it.

The following covers the packaging and release steps after development is complete.

docs.title: Final Settings
docs.text: In conf.lua, set the _RELEASED variable to true to mark the game as a release build; the game will automatically hide the console when this is enabled.

docs.title: Packaging
docs.text:
All LOVE2D game projects are packaged using the following flow:

source project -> compress into .love -> combine with love.exe

For convenience on Windows, you can use the engine's included tool LovePackager.exe (purple heart icon with an arrow).

Note: Do not run this packager from inside the project folder you are packaging. Running it inside the folder repeatedly can cause the packaged game size to grow unexpectedly (for example to multiple GBs) — repeated packaging may even lead to exponential growth in size.

In short: move or copy the project out of the original folder and run the packager from outside the project directory.

docs.title: Test Run
docs.text:
After packaging, you must run the packaged game and play through it thoroughly — not just let the logo screen show — to confirm there are no runtime errors before releasing.

The runtime environment for a packaged LOVE2D game can differ slightly from your development environment; those small differences can sometimes cause crashes or failures.

Don't panic — in my experience these issues are often caused by small mistakes (for example, a single-letter case error in a filename).

docs.title: Release
docs.text:
I won't go into detail on distribution platforms here.

At minimum, credit the engine. If you want to also name the engine author, you may do so, but listing the engine alone is fine.

P.S. — If you release something, mention me; I enjoy seeing what people build with the engine.

docs.title: Lua Errors
docs.text: During development, the most common issues you'll encounter are Lua runtime errors (since you're writing Lua code). To quickly find an error on this page, press Ctrl + F (or use your browser/mobile search) and enter a few keywords to locate the message and likely cause.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
These errors are usually caused by simple mistakes or oversight.

  • attempt to call a non-function value Attempting to call a value that is not a function, e.g. local x = 123; x().

  • attempt to index a nil value Attempting to index a nil value, e.g. local t = nil; print(t.key).

  • attempt to perform arithmetic on a string value Trying to do arithmetic on a string, e.g. local s = "hello"; print(s + 5).

  • unexpected symbol near '...' Syntax error: an unexpected token in the code (usually missing parentheses or a typo).

  • 'end' expected (to close 'function' at line ...) A block is missing its closing end (common in function, if, for, etc.).

  • bad argument #1 to '...' (expected ..., got ...) A function received an argument of the wrong type, e.g. string.len() called with a non-string.

  • cannot use '...' outside a vararg function The vararg ... syntax was used outside of a vararg function.

  • 'then' expected near '...' An if statement is missing the then keyword, e.g. if x > 5 print(x).

  • attempt to compare number with string Comparing a number with a string, e.g. if 5 == "5" then.

  • attempt to concatenate a nil value Concatenating nil, e.g. local x = nil; print("value: " .. x).

  • syntax error near '...' General syntax errors caused by typos, missing symbols, or malformed structures.

  • cannot call method '...': a nil value Attempting to call a method that doesn't exist on an object, e.g. local obj = {}; obj:undefinedMethod().

docs.title: Threshold / Limit Errors
docs.text:
Note: LOVE2D's Lua environment is generally permissive, so these errors are rare. However, if you encounter any of them, the usual fix is to consolidate related variables into tables (so you use fewer top-level variables).

Example: 

-- Ten separate variables
local time1, time2, time3, ...

-- Better: a single table holding the values
local time = {t1 = 0, t2 = 0, t3 = 0, ...}

Avoid having too many variables in the global scope, a single file, or within a function. Common limit/threshold errors include:

  • too many upvalues in function The function captures more external local variables (upvalues) than Lua allows.

  • too many local variables in function A function defines more local variables than the implementation limit.

  • too many constants in function The number of constants used in a function exceeds Lua's internal constant table limit.

  • control structure too long Extremely long if...elseif chains or excessively large switch/case-like constructs.

  • function or expression too complex Code nesting or expressions are too complex.

  • too many syntax levels Nesting depth (loops/conditionals) exceeds allowed levels.

  • table overflow Initializing a table with too many elements.

  • string length overflow A string literal exceeds Lua's maximum allowed length.

  • parser stack overflow The parser's internal stack overflows while parsing your code.

  • too many vararg functions An excessive number of vararg functions in the script.

  • too many captures in pattern A pattern (for string matching) contains more capture groups than allowed.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text: • Could not open file xxxx.png. Does not exist. This indicates a missing resource (image, audio, file, etc.). Double-check your file paths and ensure the resource is present in the expected location.
